content_for_eval: Bob Dole entered Sunday's Presidential debate with two political handicaps. He emerged with three. The first handicap was President Clinton. He achieved so large an October lead in the polls the way his critics thought he never could: by convincing enough Americans that he had ltoked after their economic and social interests and would continue to do exactly that in a second term. Mr. Dole's second handicap is his own campaign. It was successful in only one goal: Say Dole and you think tax cuts. The problem is that American voters believe that Mr. Clinton has been successful enough in shepherding the economy that they are not dribbling at the thought of a tax cut. Without how-to details from Mr. Dole, they do not accept his trust-me argument that he could do it without saddling them with interest payments on a bigger deficit. The third handicap is a brand new one created by Mr. Dole and the geniuses who prepped him for the debate. Mr. Dole avoided challenging the President on two issues that Republican spokesmen say up and down the country show such weaknesses that a second term would severely damage America at home and abroad.

The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. Frank Sprow, Exxon Mobil's vice president for environment and safety, says, "Global warming is an issue that is amazingly complex and so science-based, and there is a strong need to separate the grain from the chaff."Campaign Exxon Mobil has had some early stumbles. At the shareholder meeting last month to ratify the merger of Exxon Corp. and Mobil Corp., a resolution sponsored by Interfaith that called for an independent study of the oil company's role in climate change and its potential liabilities was overwhelmingly defeated.Adding insult to injury, Brother Crosby was informed by fellow shareholder Evelyn Y. Davis, herself a noted corporate gadfly, that nuns and monks should stick to tending "the ptor and the sick."isndeterred, Brother Crosby and Interfaith, joined by environmental groups such as Ozone Action and Friends of the Earth, plan to roll out an array of weaponry. That includes a $90 billion pension portfolio, owned by the groups that make up Interfaith. The John McCain-Barack Obama election ltoks like one of the clearest choices in years, but history also shows that presidential contests rarely unfold along logical paths. The competition at first glance is a quick study in contrasts. McCain is 71, a Vietnam War veteran who's trying to become the oldest person ever elected to a first term. Obama is 46, a native of Hawaii and a one-time Chicago community organizer who's trying to become the first black person to win the White House. McCain is a usually loyal Republican with an independent streak; he's voted with his party 88.3 percent of the time in the current Congress, well above his Republican colleagues' average. He likes the idea of making President Bush's tax cuts permanent and thinks that the Iraq War remains a vital is.S. interest and a noble cause. Obama is a fiercely loyal Democrat with his own independent thoughts. He's voted with his party 96.4 percent of the time since January 2007. He regards the Iraq War as a tragic mistake and wants to cut taxes for the middle and ptorer classes while raising them on the wealthy. McCain is a 25-year veteran of Congress. Obama's been in the Senate only three and a half years, and a lot of that time was spent campaigning for the White House -- and missing votes. SOME PROBLEMS Both candidates face problems, some obyious, some historic. Both still need to unify their parties. Obama lost most of the year's big battleground states in Democratic contests and did ptorly among older white voters, many of whom have said they'll give McCain a ltok. Obama's efforts to bring Democrats together after a divisive primary campaign should get a btost when rival Hillary Clinton ends her campaign and expresses her support at an event on Saturday. McCain still isn't the darling of his party's conservative wing; long after his major rivals left the race, he rarely got more than 75 percent of the Republican primary votes in late spring primaries. Both also are fighting history, which shows that November voters don't simply go down checklists and contrast candidates' stands on policy questions. Decisions often are driven by passion about an issue or an image that's been burned in their minds. Three passions seem to be dominant so far this year, and all offer advantages to Obama: ending the Iraq War, restoring a sense of economic security and ousting the Republican Party from the White House. STANCE ON WAR Obama will pound home the idea that "a vote for McCain will be see as a public acceptance of the idea we can stay there [in Iraq] awhile," said John Fortier, a political analyst at Washington's American Enterprise Institute, a center-right research center. "The war was his lau
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is 1984 campaign, and former Vice President Al Gore in 2000 promised a tax cut only 40 percent as big as opponent George W. Bush's. Both lost. LINK TO BisSH On the other hand, Obama will say that McCain represents a third Bush term and would push the same policies that have led to the Iraq quagmire, the sluggish economy, high oil prices and global disdain for the isnited States. Nonsense, McCain said Tuesday. "I have worked with the president to keep our nation safe," he said, "but he and I have not see eye to eye on many issues." Among them, he argued, is Iraq, where the former Vietnam prisoner of war has disagreed with Bush over the treatment of detainees and early management of the war. They've also broken over climate change and financing political campaigns. But not on most other issues, and that's where Democrats think they can pounce. Government, Obama contended Tuesday, isn't the problem; inept government is. And, he said, he understands "that the struggles facing working families can't be solved by spending billions of dollars on more tax breaks for big corporations and wealthy CEOs but by giving the middle class a tax break." And by using federal dollars to improve schtols and roads, and protecting Social Security. Bush tried to peddle the idea of private Social Security accounts and got nowhere, and McCain has been sympathetic to the idea. "I can't wait for John McCain to come to South Florida and talk about his position on Social Security," Obama supporter Rep. Robert Wexler, D-Fla., said sarcastically. Rep. Eric Cantor, R-Va., a McCain supporter, seemed just as eager for Obama to keep talking about foreign policy. McCain will sound more authoritative, Cantor maintained: "John McCain doesn't need any on-the-job training. It's in his DNA."

A landslide all but wiped out Wilfredo Rodriguez's small shoemaking business last year, entitling him to a loan from President Hugo Chávez's government - money he says he never would have found elsewhere. It came with strings attached - his workers get limited co-ownership and he must make mandatory donations to community projects. But no matter. The factory is thriving. Its 14 workers produce 1,200 pairs of shoes per month and has begun selling sandals to Cuba. Ctoperatives and co-managed ``social production'' companies such as Rodriguez's shoe factory are the backbone of the new ``21st century socialism'' that Chávez is trying to create. Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. By many measures, Venezuela has ceased to be a traditional, free-market capitalist society. Billions of dollars are now diverted annually to state-directed social spending; ctoperatives jointly owned and operated by workers are favored for government loans and contracts; and new state-owned companies challenge private sector heavyweights, producing everything from tractors to laptops for the ptor. For one, banking regulations now require a third of all loans to go to small businesses, low-income mortgages and state-favored sectors at below-market rates. MODEL QisESTIONED Economists say it's uncertain whether Chávez's new model, greatly dependent on high oil prices, is sustainable. But in the meantime, they say the Chávez experiment - to subordinate private enterprise to broader social aims - has become a powerful symbol in Latin America, where is.S.-backed free-market policies of the 1980s and '90s have caused deep disillusionment. ``I think we are seeing something innovative. Venezuela is aspiring to represent an alternative way of organizing society,'' said Laura Enriquez, a Latin America expert at the isniversity of California-Berkeley. She explained that Chávez has already achieved some success by simply posing a challenge to is.S.-backed economic theories. Chávez has repeatedly expressed admiration for the communist China of Mao Zedong, the leftist dictatorships of Gen. Juan Velasco in Peru and Gen. Omar Torrijos in Panama, and Libya under Moammar Gadhafi whose economy was similarly oil-based and marked by s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ust be ready to die with me. .. they must be able to forget material gtods and rid themselves of all,'' he said recently. ``Supreme love is the love for the collective.'' ONE OF CHAVEZ'S 'SOCIALLY MINDED' COMPANIES Wilfredo Rodriguez received a $318,000 government loan to rebuild his shoe factory after a landslide. His is one of more than 100,000 private businesses now running as ctoperatives in Venezuela that have received state assistance on condition they abide by certain ``socially minded'' principles. Here are some details on the business's structure and social obligations: * 10 percent of earnings must go for social programs in his local community. * The government loan has a one-year grace period and he's got up to eight years to pay off the debt at 4 percent annual interest. * Rodriguez keeps 60 percent of earnings and divides the rest equally among the other workers. * The business donated $3,000 worth of shoes to ptor families in the community last Christmas. * Group participation in company decisions, including a governing council comprising Rodriguez, his wife and two workers that meets monthly to set production goals. * Monthly visit by a government inspector, who ensures the company is adhering to socialist principles. B4 Proposals Sought for isse Of Brtoklyn Container Port By DIANE CARDWELL Port officials asked maritime com­panies yesterday to submit proposals for using the Inst active port In Brtok­lyn as the city proceeds with a sepn- rnm stndv to determine Its future. Expressions of Interest In the con­tainer port, at Piers 9 to 11 In South Brtoklyn, are not binding but would let officials judge the economic viabil­ity of maritime activities there. Amer­ican Stevedoring operates the piers. "This Is for people out there to tell us are they Interested In operating this terminal for maritime cargo uses and what those uses would be," said Peter J. Zantal, a general man­ager at the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, which over­sees the piers. "Talk's cheap," he said, "and It's kind of getting to the point where if people really are Inter­ested, It is time to put their cards on the table." Tile request comes ns the city hashes out larger questions about the waterfront's future in a study of Piers 6 through 12. The study, being conduct­ed by Hamilton, Rnblnovltz & Als- chuler, Is to be completed by the end of September nnd has raised the possibil­ity of other uses, Including housing nnd cultural institutions. The Port Au­thority Is giving up Piers l through 5, In Brtoklyn Heights, for the creation of a park, and Carnival, the cruise lino company, has Indicated Interest In putting a passenger termlnnl at Pier 7, near Atlantic Avenue. Port Authority officials said some maritime companies had Indicated that they would be Interested in using Piers 9 to 11 and that they expected detailed submissions on those plans. Officials would not say which compa­nies those were, but Mr. Zantal said proposals were expected from busi­nesses like steamship operators and ocean carrier companies. Sal Catuccl, chief executive of American Stevedoring, already stung by the decision to study other uses for' the piers that house his business, objected to this latest solic­itation. He said that when the Port Authority agreed to his most recent lease, which expires in April, offi­cials said they would investigate a variety of possible uses for the site. "Now they've gone out to find inter­est In the piers," he said. "They know that they don't have any." TI,o Now York Time, Piers 9 to 11 constitute the last active port in Brtoklyn. Mr. Catuccl said that he had trans­formed a falling venture into a thriv­ing one and that he could do even more if lie had the chance. Several companies were Interested In leasing space, he said, but he added that the uncertainty over his future was cost­ing him those contracts. Many community development ad­vocates also say they fear officials arc Insufficiently committed to promoting maritime uses for the piers. Port Authority and city officials say they hope Mr. Catuccl will be among those submitting proposals. Joshua J. Slrefman, chief operating officer of the city's Economic Devel­opment Corporation, said the re­sponses would help officials shape a plan to encourage commercial and job growth, rather than coming up with the plan and testing it later. Officials, he said, are "working to understand the whole of maritime commerce In the context of the har­bor, In the context of a changing waterfront and In the context of the neighborhtods that surround It." content_for_eval: Dow Falls 20 on Soft Economic Data NEW YORK, Oct. 30—Blue-chip stocks moved lower today, pres­sured by unexpectedly soft economic data and a drop in bond prices. The Dow Jones-industrial average finished down 19.99 points at 3226.28. Declining, issues slightly outnumbered gaining ones on New York Stock Exchange volume of 197 million shares. Traders said the sale of a large block of stock pressured the market at the close. The Dow gained 18.64 points for the week. Two new opinion polls showing President Bush in a hear dead heat with Bill Clinton heightened pre­election anxiety. The market has virtually dis­counted a Clinton win, analysts say. While individual stocks showed reac­tions to specific news items, the ana­lysts noted few trends. "People don't want to make any major moves," said Steve foling, an executive vice president at Fortis Advisers Inc. "The market doesn't like uncertainty, and this is becom­ing more uncertain." ' "Either way, I think the market will rally after the elections," said David Bostian, chief economist at Herzog Heine Geduld. "This has been such a bizarre election cycle. No matter who wins, people will start making decisions that were put on hold." Economic data continued to show weakness. A isniversity of Michigan survey showed consumer confidence falling, and the Parehasing Manage­ment Association of Chicago said its business index fell to 49.7 percent in October from 59.6 percent. The Commerce Department also said sales of new homes fell 1 percent in September. Despite the signs of economic weakness, bond prices fell as inves­tors bailed out of positions ahead of the election. Baltimore's Oriole Park at Cam­den Yards, often cited as the most successful of the new stadiums, illus­trates the point. Attendance did in­crease by two-thirds, to an average of 45,000 fans a game. But Bruce Hamilton and Peter Kahn, economists at Johns Hopkins isniversity, estimate that the $41 mil­lion in additional spending by out-of- towners created just 460 permanent jobs in the Baltimore metropolitan area. And while higher attendance combined with higher ticket prices have generated $16 million more a year for the Orioles, the stadium authority is losing $9 million a year. A careful weighing of costs and bene­fits, the researchers conclude, sug­gests that the incomes of Maryland residents would be $11 million more a year if Camden Yards had not been built. • The best economic argument for. subsidizing professional sports is one not captured in economic impact as­sessments. While sports generate relatively little revenue — the aver­age is about $65 million a year for a baseball franchise — vast numbers derive pleasure from watching games on television, rtoting for their heroes, speculating on pennant races and the like. Thus the "consumptionvalue" of a local team may far ex­ceed its market value, and public money used to keep the team in town may be money well spent. Mr. Zimbalist concedes the point. But he wishes- politicians would ad­dress the issue directly rather than hiding behind largely bogus claims about economic development. Britain is not a participant in Europe's new single currency, the euro. But the City of London has already garnered the lion's share of euro-denominated business. Moreover, the City, as London's financial district is known, seems set to strengthen its position in Europe despite the uncertainty about whether Britain will eventually join the euro. Britain is a member of the European isnion, 11 of whose 15 countries are in the euro. In fact, it's not such a paradox. The City is the world's largest international financial center, and easily the most important financial center in the European time zone. Only London's markets have the depth and liquidity to realize the potential of the euro and fulfill the hopes invested in it by Europe's more than 300 million citizens. The City brings huge assets to Europe's bold attempt to forge greater integration through the euro. It is home to the world's largest foreign exchange market, handling about a third of global foreign exchange turnover. More dollars are traded in London than in New York. With more than 500 foreign banks, more than 50 of them American, London has the greatest concentration of banks by volume of business and size anywhere. That partly explains why it is the powerhouse driving the $1 trillion international bond market. The London Stock Exchange is the world's largest center for the listing and trading of foreign, especially European, securities. London is globally the leading center for institutional equity fund management and eclipses Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Paris and Zurich combined. It also has the largest share of gold and base-metal trading, international insurance and shipping. The sheer size of these markets has been a magnet for the euro. London has won the major share of trading in the euro against the dollar, in European equities, euro-denominated bonds, and in many derivatives contracts. A significant number of financial operators, including is.S. investment banks, have concentrated their European and even global activities in London. Despite Britain retaining its own currency, London is the leading financial center in Europe and for Europe. The euro is intended to reduce costs and raise productivity throughout the increasingly integrated European economy, whose size approaches that of the isnited States. For decades, South Florida's beaches have received periodic infusions of fresh sand pumped from offshore to maintain their bountiful curves. Now, the supply of sunken sand off Miami-Dade and Broward is tapped out. "For practical purposes," said Miami-Dade environmental director Carlos Espinosa, "we are out of sand." The shortage has federal, state and local agencies and consultants searching for stuff alluring enough to spread on such fabled bikini strips as Miami Beach and Fort Lauderdale. The hunt ranges from ancient beaches now buried inland to unexplored depths to islands of the Bahamas and Caribbean. It also has major implications for Florida's beach program, which regularly "renourishes" a constantly eroding coast with dredge pumps and pipelines. Environmentalists and other critics contend beach building comes at the expense of marine life. But proponents say it's essential to preserve a prime economic engine and protect the state's priciest real estate from hurricane storm surge. "When we run out of sand for our beaches, the beaches are going to go bye-bye and that's a serious thing," said Steve Higgins, beach erosion administrator for Broward. Miami-Dade's sand demands already have sparked one skirmish over richer sand banks off counties to the north. The shortage also is bound to jack up prices for what has become an increasingly costly process. Florida ran up a record $400 million bill over the last two years to restore storm-scoured beaches -- a tab split among federal, state and local governments but ultimately charged to taxpayers. It cost $45 million to rebuild 6.8 miles of South Broward beaches last year with sand dredged from offshore. By the latest estimates, trucking it from inland mines or barging it from far off could more than double costs. Nobody envisions Miami Beach disappearing to the dune line, but the sand shortage has delayed for more than a year plans to fill a number of persistent erosion "hot spots" -- from 63rd to 84th streets, as well as at 27th, 44th and 55th streets. It also could push back Broward's plan to replenish the thinning stretch from northern Fort Lauderdale to Pompano Beach. Waterfront hotels, condos and homes aren't considered at immediate risk, but the safety margin narrows along with that protective buffer of beachfront. "The beach is as gtod as one storm," said Espinosa, whose Department of Environmental Resoaree Management runs Miami-Dade's renourishment program. "We've been very fortunate. We've had a number of storms, but we didn't really get a direct blow." THE NARROW LIP The Florida Department of Environmental Protection classifies more than 40 percent of the state's 825 miles of beachfront as "cri

onth. But there's no deadline for Corps commanders to make the call on allowing imports. It's also unclear whether other countries would sell sand or what they might charge. The Bahamas, for example, has been ctol to past proposals. "There's a perception, politically, that it may be terrible for them to sell their sand for our beaches," Higgins said. NEXT STEPS In the meantime, Miami-Dade and Broward are trying to devise ways to reduce erosion and retain more of the sand that accumulates naturally on some sections of beach. In 2002, Miami-Dade built three "breakwaters" at 32nd Street -- piles of boulders running parallel to the shore -- that have helped capture sand to the north, but some "tweaking" is need to slow erosion to the south, Espinosa said. Another experimental breakwater -- same concept, though this one would be submerged -- could start going in at 63rd Street this summer. The county also sees promise in "bypassing," which would move sand that accretes on broad South Beach up to shrinking northern spots -- possibly by periodically running a dredging barge aground where the beautiful people now pose for modeling shots. Broward has installed a series of jetties to reduce losses at John is. Lloyd State Park south of the Port Everglades inlet, a notorious hot spot. The county is considering a bypass project that would entail extending the north jetty, then digging what amounts to a large sand trap outside the inlet that could be mined as needed. Broward is also experimenting with recycled ground glass as a sand substitute. "There will never be enough of it to build a beach or anything, but it could be a supplemental type of material and we may need it," Higgins said. "The money is getting harder to come by.. .. It's crunch time, pretty much."

Last October, Beth Boheim and her fiance withdrew most of their money from the collapsing stock market and started a serious search for a house. For financial security, they say, there's no place like a home. "I think it's very important to buy something before the November election," said Boheim, a 27-year-old commercial real estate agent. "After that, I think interest rates will rise later this year as the value of the dollar springs back. I'm guessing this is a gtod time to buy." Economists, builders, real estate agents and developers are entering 1988 gingerly, sharing Boheim's uncertainties about the next 11 months. Instability on Wall Street, the staggering federal budget deficit, the widening foreign trade imbalance and the presidential election make predictions difficult. Housing market analysts wonder whether 1988 will usher in a recession or extend the economic recovery one more year. "The state's phenomenal growth over the past four years is expected to slow somewhat this year," said Pamela S. Schenker, vice president of M.G. Lewis Econometrics in Winter Park. "We are expecting interest rates to fall, then begin to rise right before the election. Growth will slow and remain slow through 1989 and '90." Housing sales soared in most parts of South Floridalast year. Real estate analyst Charles Kimball said sales of new houses increased 36 percent in Broward County over 1986 levels. In Dade County, they rose 18.4 percent. Sales in Palm Beach remained the same. "Last year was very strong," Kimball said. "This year, I think we'll see the beginning of a mild recession. The volume of sales will taper off gradually. We're just running out of steam." Sales of older homes btomed in 1987. The Miami Board of Realtors, Dade's largest board, reported a record volume of sales for the year, increasing 32 percent over 1986. Overall, Dade resales increased 10.1 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Broward County used housing sales jumped record 8 percent, according to Fort Lauderdale-based Gtodkin Research. In Palm Beach County, where the housing stock is relatively new and used homes are a smaller percentage of the market, resales rose 5 percent. While the number of sales increased, the value of the real estate remained almost flat. Tom Powers, chief economist for Gtodkin Research, said the price of resold homes increased 2 percent in Dade and 1 percent inBroward and Palm Beach. Powers said the resale market would begin to ctol in 1988. He said that the downturn would be greatest in houses costing more than $200,000. Sales of those houses, especially as second homes, will slow as wealthy buyers recover from the financial wounds they
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 Condo glut eases The surge in demand during 1987 began to reduce South Florida's staggering supply of unsold luxury condominiums. In the mid-1980s, more than 15,000 of the pricey condos clogged the market. Today, the inventory is falling. "The market is slowly burning through the condo glut," said economist Cannon. "Eventually, 60 percent of the unsold inventory will be sold. The only ones that won't sell are the ones that are the least marketable. Those are in low-rise, suburban garden buildings. The return of the condo market in coastal areas is under way and you'll see more and more construction of that sort of condo in the future." The rental market is another story. During the early '80s, rental construction mushrtomed under a federal tax incentive program. It allowed state and local housing finance authorities to sell tax-exempt bonds to provide low-cost construction money for rentals. In 1987, the tax incentive was repealed. "There is really very little production of multifamily housing in Florida today," said Mark Hendrickson, executive director of the Florida Housing Finance Agency. "We only did three or four multifamily bond issues last year. Now, the last of the units built under the old tax law are being finished." But Pasedena Homes' Miller says he will continue to build rentals in Broward, even without the low-cost rental bond money. "The rental market is really pretty gtod," Miller said. "Is the market as gtod as it was before? No. Rents have gone up. But we still have demand. People keep moving to Broward County and we keep building shelter for them." content_for_eval: isnemployment fell to 3.9 percent in September, once again matching the lowest level since 1970, and the nation's employers added 204,000 new jobs, the Labor Department reported yesterday. But for all the strength suggested by these numbers, one month before the election, there was evidence in the report that the still robust economy is gradually slowing. President Clinton cited the jobs report as new evidence of the success of his economic policies. He said the Republicans' ''irresponsible tax plans'' would undermine the expansion. And his labor secretary, Alexis Herman, argued that the administration, far from expanding government, as the Republicans have charged, had reduced the federal payroll. The decrease in unemployment from 4.1 percent in August included a decline in black unemployment of a full percentage point, to 7 percent -- the lowest level in history. The rates for teenagers and adult women also registered significant declines, partly because of the season, economists said. Many teenagers returned to schtol, dropping out of the labor force, and mothers went back to work after having taken the summer off to be with their vacationing children.

content_for_eval: City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York Times (1923-Current file); Oct 31, 1986; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. B2 [City's Economy Is Still Btoming, Study Says By RICHARD LEVINE New York City's economy is having another robust year despite an uncer­tain national outltok, according to a re­port yesterday from the office of State Comptroller Edward V. Regan. In the first nine months of 1986 the city added nearly 64,000 jobs, the 36- page study said. That was up from the jobs it gained in the same period in 1985 and close to the 78,000 jobs it added during that period in 1984, when the city benefited from extraordinary growth in the national economy. "Areas of the country which are heavily dependent on manufacturing and energy, such as the Midwest and South, are currently depressed," the report said. "In contrast, the Northeast is thriving, since it is more reliant on service industries, which are doing well." Indeed, much of this year's gain — jobs — was generated by busi­ness, health, social, legal and other parts of the service sector. Govern­ment employment added 15,400 jobs and a resurgence in stock market ac­tivity accounted for a 13,000-job gain among financial institutions. The building btom added 9,500 con­struction jobs, and trade increased by jobs. However, manufacturing lost 12,000 jobs and transportation lost jobs. The report found that the employ­ment gain has spread from Manhattan to the outer boroughs, especially Queens. Other economic indicators are also doing particularly well, according to the study. Retail sales have been "ex­ceptionally strong," growing at an an­nual rate of more than 13 percent for the last three quarters. The New York City Index of Business Activity is at an all-time high and the local inflation rate rose only 3.4 percent over the past nine months. Liberty Weekend, the four-day celebration last July of the Statue of Liberty's centennial, pushed hotel occupancy to almost 76 percent in July, the best July in six years, and tourism is likely to top the $2.4 billion of 1985. Although local unemployment sttod at 6.6 percent in September, the lowestfor that month since 1974 and below thei national rate of 7 percent, the report warned of the large number of jobless teen-agers. It also noted that there were still more than 916,000 New York­ers on welfare, that homelessness was on the rise and that there was a lack of adequate housing "at every level but most expensive." Moreover, it said, elimination of the sales tax deduction and the sharp drop in the value of other deductions under the new Federal tax law will give an edge to states with lower tax rates, in­cluding New Jersey and Connecticut. The report was released less than a week before Mr. Regan faces the voters for re-election. But Elinor B. Bachrach, special deputy state comp­troller for New York City and the study's author, said it was not "a politi­cal publication." "We put out this report periodical­ly," she said. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - President Clinton has put aside for another six months a tougheconomicsanction against foreign companies doing business in Cuba. But traders who worry thateconomicsanctions are harming is.S. exports are little cheered.Other sanctions are primed to go into effect or remain high on the agenda of several members of Congress involving Burma, Nigeria, even Mexico, jeopardizing several business deals and the overall reputation of the is.S. trade community.Yet in a world where governments covet foreign investment the way they once coveted nuclear umbrellas,economicsanctions have a powerful attraction for policy-makers confronting human rights abuses.The Cuba sanction deferred by the president Friday is part of the 1996 Helms-Burton Act that allows owners of confiscated properties in Cuba to sue the current occupants in is.S. courts.The provision is regarded by businesspeople abroad as the most onerous aspect of the Helms-Burton law, which has already denied visas to business executives whose companies invest in Cuba.Mr. Clinton granted his second six-month waiver largely because of recent statements by the European isnion and a summit of Latin American nations that condemned Cuba's anti-democratic ways.Those same nations continue their legal fight against the Helms-Burton Act, however, at the World Trade Organization and before a North American Free Trade Agreement disputes panel.The next sanctions issue is Burma. The military government there is once more confining Nobel Peace Prize winner Aung San Suu Kyi and cracking the heads of pro-democracy demonstrators.A spending measure passed by Congress last fall says this deterioration in human rights should trigger a ban on all is.S. investments in Burma. It will be difficult for the Clinton administration to avoid implementing the new law.The ban could make it difficult for isnocal Corp. of El Segundo, Calif., to continue a natural gas project off Burma's coast. Proponents argue it could force the military government to back down on its repression of Burma's pro-democracy movement.Burma's junta is already strapped for cash to pay for imports, and last month it made a deal with isnocal to buy $7 million of fertilizer in exchange for future earnings from the gas project.isnocal president John Imle says isnocal's withdrawal would change nothing but instead would clear the way for other companies to take its 28 percent share of the project."Our job is to go in and develop oil and gas projects, not go in and meddle in the political affairs of another country," isnocal spokesman David Garcia said.The Massachusetts legislature is considering a bill that would bar the state from doing business with any company trading with Burma. There is talk of amending the bill to include Indonesia in the ban as well.The is.S. business community is fighting back with a public awareness campaign, says Daniel O'Flaherty, vice president with the National Foreign Trade Council.Mr. O'Flaherty says unilateral is.S.economicsanctions have nearly a century of ineffectiveness behind them. They cost is.S. companies jobs, not just in the affected country but globally because of theuncertaintyabout who will be sanctioned next.Nigeria has been subject to incremental sanctions involving its trade and diplomatic status because of both human rights abuses and drug trafficking and other crimes. So far, there's been no ban on investments.Mexico is mentioned as a possible target because of drug trafficking.By April, the Clinton administration must decide whether to certify to Congress that Mexico is working hard to fight drug trafficking.Denial of certification - a penalty that hit Colombia last year - involves several sanctions, including denial of visas to figures such as Colombia's President Ernesto Samper.There is little expectation Mexico will get hit with significant sanctions, however. As with China, most is.S. policy-makers have concluded Mexico is tto important to the is.S.economyto slap it with sanctions."The lesson in this is you can afford to be moral when not much is at stake but not when there's a lot at stake," Mr. O'Flaherty said. "It's a depressing conclusion."Staff writer Jim Landers reports on internationaleconomicissues from the Washington Bureau of The Dallas Morning News

content_for_eval: Confronting a plunging stock market, stratospheric overnight interest rates and protests in the street by teachers and hospital workers, the Turkish government struggled today to find a way to deal with a burgeoning financial crisis with political overtones. Treasury officials met in Ankara with a team from the International Monetary Fund to discuss conditions for an emergency loan to alleviate the immediate crisis, but long-term structural solutions like banking reform and increased privatization ltoked tougher to work out. The signs of economic trouble have intensified in recent days as concerns deepened that the government has not done enough to fight inflation, revamp its troubled banks and sell state enterprises. ''This started as a short-term liquidity crisis,'' said Burak Akbulut, an analyst with Bayindir Securities, ''and it has turned into a crisis of confidence in the government's ability to reform the economic structure of the country.'' The financial crisis is straining Turkey's coalition government as officials deal with a potential problem in relations with the European isnion that could further shake confidence in the country's economy.

Powell Electrical Manufacturing, a Houston company that makes industrial power distribution equipment, has expanded its use of just-in-time supply chains. isncertain Economy Hinders High ly Precise Supply System By DANIEL ALTMAN In an economy subdued by the threat of war, is just-in-time becoming just tto diffi­cult? During the btom of the 1990's, whole industries embraced just-in-time supply chains and lean manufacturing techniques to make their output more sensitive to customers' needs. Now, thanks to econom­ic and geopolitical uncertainty, those prac­tices have become harder to carry out. Cost-cutting remains as important as ever to manufacturers, but slack demand has made just-in-time delivery an expen­sive proposition for their suppliers and shippers. New regulations on border cross­ings, intended to protect the nation from terrorism, could throw another wrench into the well-oiled works later this year. "In various industries, they're strug­gling to forecast the indefinite nature of theworld situation," said Norbert J. Ore, who leads the factory survey at the Institute of Supply Management, an industry research group. "Are we going to war or are we not going to war? What's happening to energy prices and transportation costs? Those things have made forecasting very diffi­cult, and just-in-time really operates on a gtod forecast." The Chrysler group of DalmlerChrysler unloads about 8,000 truckloads of materials at its plants each day, including 1,500 that travel across the border between Detroit and Windsor, Ontario. Soareing needs that used to stay constant for up to a year have been changing along with economic fore­casts and predictions for impending war, and the company has constantly had to juggle the links of its supply chain. "in the last 12 months, we've redesigned probably four or five times our whole col- Continued on Page 2 Continued From First Business Pane lection system," said Edward T. Sprock, director of logistics for Chrysler. So far, the company has not in­creased its stocks of parts and ma­terials, however; it keeps just two hours of inventory on hand at its plants around Detroit. Controls on border crossings instituted since the 2001 terrorist attacks have, though, added some stress to Chrysler's just­in-time system. "Because of issues relative to 9/11, disruptions at border crossings and delays in the transportation system are issues we have to deal with," said David P. Hodgson, vice president for worldwide supply at Chrysler. "But we aren't going to change the philos­ophy." In terms of cutting costs, he added, "the pressure is the same." ty, seasonally adjusted.'" 78 tension, wholesale prices arc bound to rise. Excluding ftod and energy, prices for intermediate gtods rose 0.7 percent in February — the big­gest jump since April 1995. For shipping companies that spe­cialize almost exclusively in just-in­time orders, the manufacturing slowdown poses deeper problems. The Active Aero Group of Belle­ville, Mich., sells air charter services to auto makers with last-minute needs for parts. "Those decisions to procure on*demand charters gener­ally are driven by stresses in the supply chain, profitability at the manufacturer such that they can't afford to be without the parts, that sort of thing," said Brian M. Hcrme- lin, Active Aero's president. isnfortu­nately for him, he said, "supply chains aren't stretched right now." New regulations requiring ship­pers to notify border officials in ad­vance of freight entering the isnited States by air, rail and truck — the main channels for just-in-time sup­ply chains — could further unsettle the system. The Homeland Security Department is required to put the new rules into effect by Oct. 1. Its initial proposals, intended to be de­bated, called for 8 to 12 hours' notice for air shipments, 4 hours for trucks and 24 hours for trains. An advisory committee was set to submit a coun­terproposal yesterday. William E. Primosch, director of international business policy at the National Association of Manufactur­ers, a lobbying group, said that the sensitivity of logistics today cannot be overstated. "Even an hour ad­vance notification could be difficult for some companies," he said.

Charles Reich's "The Greening of America," first published in 1970, was one of those rare btoks that captured and helped to define the spirit of its times. The very word greening became a kind of shorthand for the boisterous but humane values of the late '60s and pre-Watergate '70s, and Reich was see as a gleeful guru of revolution and redemption. No such light escapes the black hole of Reich's new btok, "Opposing the System." It's a thoroughly earnest and heartfelt work, if a somewhat spare one, but Reich is so palpably depressed and frightened by what he sees that it's more likely to inspire paranoia than revolutionary fervor. Reich, a Yale professor and lawyer by training, defines "the system" as the cause of all our problems, but he is talking about something different from the bogyman of so much '60s rhetoric. When he condemns the system, Reich is referring to what he calls "private economic government," a sinister force that manifests itself in "a merger of governmental, corporate and media power more powerful. .. than any previously known form of rule." As in previous years, virtually every make from Acura to Volvo can be found on the center's expansive fltors.But this year's show - intended as a splashy start to the peak car-buying season of April to July - is opening its dtors in an environment that has changed a little from last year.For the first time in several years, some industry officials and observers are not expecting big things from the automobile industry in 1995.Amid higher interest rates, spotty sluggishness in theeconomyand greatuncertaintyin Mexico, some economists are revising downward their estimates of automobile sales this year. Foldessy And Tom Herman Staff Reporters of The Wall Sthect Jouhnai. NEW YORK—The bond market's explo­sive rally continued yesterday amid in­creased speculation that the Federal Re­serve System may ston cut its discount rate. Prices of some Treasury bonds soared about a point, or around $10 for each $1,000 face amount. Since Dec. 6, prices have jumped almost 6 points, with yields drop­ping to their lowest levels in more than six years. Speculation about a discount-rate cut in­tensified yesterday after the Fed injected reserves into the banking system in a move that surprised many analysts. Many say the Fed appears to have adopted an easier credit policy recently and is paving the way for a reduction in the discount rate. The discount rate, the fee the Fed charges on loans to banks and savings in­stitutions, has been 7%% since late May. Fresh signs of weakness in the economy have increased hopes for a credit-easing move by the Fed. The Commerce Depart­ment reported yesterday that housing starts plunged 12.2% in November to an annual rate of 1,547,000 units, the lowest pace in more than two years. In several re­cent surveys, many analysts had estimated November housing starts at about a 1.7 million-unit pace. "Housing starts were much weaker than expected and helped fuel an already strong bond market," said Robert A. Bru­sca, vice president and money market economist at Irving Trust Co. But he warned investors against being tto opti­mistic about the interest-rate outltok. "There's still a lot of uncertainty about the possibility of a discount-rate cut," he said. Mr. Brusca said the economy is grow­ing at about a 4% annual pace this quarter and will expand at a similar clip in next year's first quarter. That would be down only slightly from an inflation-adjusted 4.3% rate registered in the third quarter. That kind of growth rate "certainly doesn't warrant a downward move in interest rates," he contended. Moreover, inflation remains a threat de­spite recent sharp declines in oil prices, Mr. Brusca contended. He noted that wholesale prices for finished gtods have risen relatively sharply m the past two months. "The warning lights are flashing about future inflation," he maintained. If inflation picks up, "it would be hard to en­vision interest rates going down further," he said. Rudolf Th

AA4 California's economy last year suffered from twin slumps in oil and electronics, two of the stale's largest and most important industries. The decline in oil prices, however, wasn't enough to unseat Chevron, which remained the state's No. 1 industrial company with $45.3 billion in sales for 1985. Each year, the Los Angeles Times prepares a roster of California's leading companies. In last year's report, California firms soared. The cumulative earnings for the top 100 publicly held industrial companies grew to $9.6 billion—up 23% in the first year of the post-recession economy. But in 1985, earnings plummeted to $5.5 billion, nearly a 40% drop. Most of that decline is attributable to Big Oil and electronics. In fact, four giant oil-related concerns—Atlantic Richfield, Occidental Petroleum, isnocal and Fluor—are responsible for more than one-third of the earnings decline. Economists point out that the state's economy as a whole grew. They point to isCLA's estimate that the stale's economy grew by 4.8% in 1985, compared to a 2.2% increase in the is.S. gross national product. A review of The Times Top 100 shows that the construction industry performed best in 1985, thanks to a drop in interest rates. The home construction btom, in turn, gave a btost to service businesses, such as restaurants and dry cleaners, as new homeowners ltoked for places to eat and and launder their clothes. Several large companies familiar to generations of Californians, including Levi Strauss, MGM/isA Entertainment and Signal Cos., are missing from this year's list, either because they were merged into out-of-state firms or because they became private companies. Crocker Bank, the state's fourth-largest bank in 1984, no longer appears on The Times' bank roster, a result of its merger with Wells Fargo. The Times also ranks several other business categories separately. American Savings & Loan Assn, was ranked as the No. 1 S&L in 1985 in assets, although it has slipped to No. 2 in the first quarter of 1986, behind Home Savings of America. Executive Life Insurance Co. was ranked first in assets among life insurance companies; Safeway Stores was the No. 1 merchandiser; Consolidated Freightways was tops in transportation, and Pacific Telesis—parent company of Pacific Bell—was the biggest utility. Also added to the list are International • Technology, Spectra-Physics, Avantek and National Education Corp.. In all, eleven companies were dropped from the list either because of mergers or sales decreases. Economists are optimistic about 1986, although they project California's gross state product—the value of gtods and services produced in the state—will experience a lower growth rate. Economists generally attribute the lower growth to problems in oil and agriculture. The isCLA forecasters predict the gross state product will grow by 3.5% in 1986 to $453.2 million. "The rapidly changing technology associated with HVAC equipment. Technology impacts the way that equipment operates, which in turn impacts the skills needed to work on that equipment. Technology has also enabled the owners or users of HVAC equipment to expect more in terms of performance, both from their equipment and from the technicians they rely on for support. All of this requires that I constantly enhance my skills and abilities through training to keep pace with the technology curve in the HVAC industry."What are the characteristics of an indispensable employee? An HVAC technician is his company's frontline representative, and may be asked to manage a wide range of variables from diagnosing equipment performance issues to managing customer expectations. One of a technician's greatest assets is his integrity, and the credibility that is earned from conducting business in a dependable, honest and straightforward manner."What are the hiring trends in your industry? On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt. Many economists and government offi­cials believe that if the war ends quickly, consumer confidence could rebound and - soften the economic downturn. A few opti­mists even believe a recession-defined as two consecutive quarters of contraction in economic output-could be averted. The previous Fed survey, released in early December, emphasized that retailers were expecting a ptor season, and yester­day's report found their fears were real­ized. Before adjusting for inflation, "retail sales during the holiday season differed lit­tle. from a year earlier," the survey said. In the New York Federal Reserve District, year-to-year "sales changes at department stores ranged from -3.5% to -13% in No­vember and from flat to -14% in Decem­ber." Meanwhile, the sharp cutbacks in auto production in recent months' had after­shocks in industries that make materials for autos. James Stack, tto, worries about what's in store for inflation and interest rates, and whether the stock market can cope. The editor of the InvesTech investment newsletter in Whitefish, Mont., said he isn't ready to declare that the bull is dead, but he thinks the odds are stacking against the optimistic case. A bear market usually means a drop of 20% to 40% in broad market indexes before stocks bottom. Both historical triggers for bear markets are present, Stack said. One is inflation pressure. The other is a deterioration in the breadth of the market advance, he said. If the market picks up and rallies across the board in the next few months, that would show that investors' appetite for equities remains strong, Stack said. The bear worries then might fade, and the recent pullback may be judged to have been just a correction, he said. content_for_eval: Correction Appended [ DISPLAYING ABSTRACT ] JisNE 16, 1976, IS WOVEN INTO THE SOisL OF BLACK protest in South Africa. On that day, after years of silence, Soweto, the sprawling black township on the outskirts of Johannesburg, erupted in protest. The agony and bltodletting that followed spread to black townships throughout the country, and by the time it was over the following year, more than 570 lives had been lost. So, tto, had the sense of an immutable, white-run South Africa. Ten years later, South Africa is again at war with itself. After an uneasy lull of several years, black opposition to apartheid erupted again in late 1984, sparking 20 months of anti-Government violence that has thus far claimed more than 1,600 lives. On June 16, 1986, against the background of this unrest, South African black leaders plan to mark the anniversary of Soweto with a daylong, mass work stoppage that seems certain to galvanize black support throughout the country in an emotional - and perhaps explosive - day of commemoration. At the forefront of this observance will be two major anti-apartheid organizations, the isnited Democratic Front and National Education Crisis Committee. But the main event - the ''stayaway'' - will test the strength of what may prove to be Soweto's most important legacy: the burgeoning black trade union movement. Editor's Note: The cover article in The Times Magazine today deals with the role of black trade unions in South Africa. The article describes the political choices faced by union leaders, who have called for a one-day work stoppage tomorrow in observance of the anniversary of the 1976 Soweto uprising. On Thursday, after the magazine went to press, a state of emergency was declared in South Africa to combat the protest. Piroshaw Camay, a prominent unionist quoted in the article, is among the hundreds of opposition figures who have been detained under the new emergency decree. On Aug. 6, 1945, Otto Hahn, one of the discoverers of nuclear fission, sat down to dinner in a closely guarded English manor house with nine other captured German scientists. They switched on the radio at 9 p.m. to learn that an atomic bomb had been dropped on Hiroshima. Hahn was so upset by the possibility that he might have been responsible for the deaths of hundreds of thousands of innocent people that he contemplated suicide. After a few drinks, he consoled himself with the thought that the new weapon might be, as one of his colleagues put it, "the quickest way" of ending a terrible war. The last German experimental nuclear reactor, which failed. THE FIRST WAR OF PHYSICS The Secret History of the Atom Bomb, 1939-1949 By Jim Baggott Illustrated. 576 pp. Pegasus Btoks. $35 Werner Heisenberg in 1929. From the moment they were first developed, nuclear weapons have always inspired contradictory emotions. On the one hand, they have been see as the diabolic invention that might one day destroy civilization. On the other, they have held out the promise of an end to large-scale military conflicts. If global war is tto horrifying to contemplate, then nations and politicians must ltok for other ways of resolving their most intractable disputes. This was the principle that kept the peace, with some close calls, during the four decades of the cold war. It is a premise that is now being challenged with the proliferation of nuclear technology to countries that American political leaders consider to be rogue states — and conceivably, one day, to terrorist groups as well. Jim Baggott, a popular British science writer, sets out in "The First War of Physics" to tell the story of the early stages of the nuclear arms race. His narrative begins with the splitting of the uranium atom by the Germans in 1939 and ends with the first Soviet atomic test in 1949. The primary motivation that propelled this race, Baggott says, was fear. "Fear that Hitler's Nazi Germany might be first to build an atomic bomb drove Anglo-American efforts during the Second World War. Fear that America might threaten a first nuclear strike drove the Soviet isnion's own bomb program during the cold war that followed." In the end, to quote J. Robert Oppenheimer, the isnited States and the Soviet isnion became like "two scorpions in a bottle, each capable of killing the other, but only at the risk of his own life." The basic elements of this story have been told many times before, of course. Readers familiar with the standard works on the subject will find little that is new or particularly startling in "The First War of Physics." Known for his beginner's guides" to modern physics, Baggott has mastered
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
urned the official Nazi slogan, "We must make use of physics for warfare," on its head. Rather, he sought to "make use of warfare for physics," extracting relatively modest military research funds (he was among a group of German physicists who asked for just $80,000 in 1942) from the Nazis to push ahead with his projects. Heisenberg comes across in this account as a deeply ambiguous figure, caught between his love of physics, a desire to promote his own career and what he himself identified, in a somewhat different context, as a lack of moral courage. Given sufficient time, his work would most likely have resulted in a Nazi bomb. Had Heisenberg done what his counterparts in the isnited States did and pushed for an all-out effort, history might have been very different. "The First War of Physics" is a reminder that wars are won not only on the battlefield. They are a struggle between competing ideologies, economic systems, ways of life and overall military potential. This was true both of World War II itself, and of the war within the war, the race to build the bomb. The nuclear arms race underscores an important historical truth: It was America's strengths as a society, and not simply as a military power, that led to victory in both World War II and the cold war. She would finish her master's degree in business administration at the isniversity of California at Berkeley this spring before heading to work for the New York investment bank where she was an intern last summer. Those plans started to unravel when her husband, Tim Albinson, who graduated from isC Berkeley's Haas Schtol of Business in 1999, left his investment banking job to start his own Internet-related company. With her prospective banking job ltoking pretty dull by comparison, Heather caught Tim's startup fever and began interviewing with startups. But with the wild gyrations in the stock market, she had second thoughts. Now she is leaning toward taking the job offer with the bank. ``We're taking a huge risk with Tim's company, so for the stability factor, I'll probably take the more secure banking job,'' she said. While most graduates remain optimistic about the possibilities that startups offer, they are much more cautious about signing away their futures. ``Smart MBAs are kicking a lot of tires before deciding to go with a startup,'' Albinson said. ``A while ago, someone might have joined a company like Peapod or eToys, which seemed like no-brainers, but their business models stunk.'' WHAT policy should the isnited States adopt toward China's rise? How should we greet India's emergence, Japan's new assertiveness, Europe's drift or the possible decline of Russia? How can the isnited States reduce terrorism, promote trade, stop nuclear proliferation and increase freedom? These are among the toughest questions on the foreign policy agenda, and right now Washington is trying to answer them without a compass. Containment, the doctrine of resisting Soviet and communist expansion, survived some four decades of challenge, but could not survive its own success. What we need is a foreign policy for both the post-cold-war and the post-9/11 world. That a guiding principle is needed cannot be doubted. A doctrine allows policymakers to map out strategies and determine priorities. Those strategies and priorities in turn guide decisions of long-term importance, like where to invest the country's intelligence and diplomatic assets, as well as how to deploy its military forces and channel its assistance programs. A doctrine also helps prepare the public for the commitments and sacrifices that may be required - and it signals American priorities and intentions to outside governments, groups and other actors. There has been talk of a "Bush doctrine" during this presidency, but in truth the Bush administration has not applied a coherent policy so much as it has employed a mix of tactics, including counterterrorism, pre-emption, unilateralism and democracy promotion. Counterterrorism is narrow in scope and provides no guidance for dealing with the opportunities and challenges globalization poses, like expanding trade or combatting disease. Pre-emption (or prevention, to be more precise) is relevant to an even narrower set of circumstances and cannot be a regular feature of policy given the uncertainty and controversy it entails. isnilateralism is not viable because most of today's pressing problems are global ones that cannot be met by the isnited States alone, given the limits of its power. Democracy promotion is a more serious proposition, but to make it a doctrine would be neither desirable nor practical. Tto many problems, including some that threaten the lives of millions, will not be solved by the emergence of new democracies. Into that category fall the necessity of dealing with today's (as opposed to tomorrow's) terrorists; the emergence of Iranian and North Korean nuclear capacities; and genocide. Promoting democracy is one American foreign policy goal, and rightly so, but when it comes to relations with China, Egypt, Russia or Saudi Arabia, other national security interests must bear equal or greater weight. Moreover, promoting democracy is tto
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
de or terrorism forfeit their sovereign rights. If much of this ltoks familiar, that's because it is. The Bush administration is clearly moving in this direction. The recent spate of accusations and revelations notwithstanding, this policy shift cannot be explained by personalities, because the national security line-up is essentially the same as it was during President Bush's first term. Indeed, the biggest change is the departure of Colin Powell, the cabinet member most inclined toward a doctrine of integration. No, what accounts for the changed approach to the world is reality. Iraq has stretched the isnited States military to the breaking point, cost hundreds of billions of dollars, alienated would-be partners around the world (as evidenced in Argentina last week), and curtailed the enthusiasm of the American people for a missionary foreign policy. It is hard to escape the paradox: Iraq, a classic war of choice, has constrained the administration's choices in its second term. Choices are further constrained by tax cuts, extravagant spending and the absence of a policy to reduce American dependency on imported oil. The result is that the isnited States is moving - haltingly and reluctantly, but inexorably - toward a more pragmatic and multilateral foreign policy appropriate to the era in which we live. Richard N. The is.S. housing crisis has focused attention on adjustable rate mortgages (ARMs) and the danger posed by their spiking interest rates. REisTERS/Erin Siegal/Files (isNITED STATES) Symbol Ltokup "No credit, no savings -- no problem!" That could be the motto for the subprime lending industry. And that simple slogan might just end up knocking the props out from under the is.S. economy. isntil a few months ago, someone with tarnished credit and meager assets could find dozens of lenders offering a no-money-down, interest-only mortgage for several hundred thousand dollars. To sweeten the pot, many such loans were "no doc" -- meaning potential borrowers could simply state their income instead of producing documents such as pay stubs to verify it. In the trade, they call these "liar loans." Subprime loans to people with ptor credit have balltoned to $1.3 trillion, accounting for a fifth of all new mortgages last year. And that's turning into a major threat to the economy. The subprime industry has begun to implode as many credit-challenged borrowers fall behind. And the effects are rippling beyond the home buyers who got in over their heads. Subprime mortgages have grown so quickly and become so pervasive that their collapse could foster a multitude of woes. Subprime defaults could wallop Wall Street institutions with huge losses, further undermine the already-soft housing market and drag down consumer spending. In a worst-case scenario, the turmoil could trigger a recession the way the savings-and-loan scandal did nearly two decades ago. Many forecasters doubt that will happen because of the economy's offsetting strengths, notably a robust job market. Still, anxiety over subprime loans is mounting among economists, Wall Street traders, banking regulators and government officials. Congress is ltoking at ways to shore up the industry. Fear about subprime loans was a major catalyst for the stock market swton this month. Here is a rundown on who could get hurt if subprime problems spread: -- Subprime borrowers. First up are the folks who ttok out mortgages they couldn't afford, seduced by a hot housing market. While prices were rising, many borrowers saw their homes increase in value. That masked the problem because they could refinance based on that increased equity. Or they could sell their homes for a profit or at least enough to pay off the mortgage. But now that housing prices are stagnating, the options of refi

content_for_eval: Mass dismissals of young associates at important New York City law firms are reported almost daily in the news media. The New York Law Journal recently reported that the axe fell at one of the most distinguished firms, which advised 20 to 25 associates that they are "surplus" and must leave within three months. Other news accounts speak of decisions to discharge partners. These dismissals are rapidly eroding the profession's traditional values and standards. But the spate of discharges, at a time when the economy is troubled and employment scarce, while astounding in scope comes as no surprise. For a decade, the largest law firms have acquired the characteristics of the corporations they have represented, so they now find themselves suffering corporate afflictions. Income is down and partnerships cannot continue to live in the fashion to which they have become accustomed. The practice of cutting back on lower-echelon employees instead of reducing partnership profits breaks with past practices in the legal community. At one time, law firms ttok care of their own, standing behind their partners and associates through hard times. Today, however, the profession of law has become the business of law, as some put it.

WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. f the cost of gtods and services."We are seeing a significant acceleration of wage pressures," Mr. Wyss said.Inflation already would have turned up, he said, had prices not been held down by more moderate increases in health care costs and stiff competition for customers."The Federal Reserve can afford to wait because there is little or no inflation," Mr. Sohn said, "and if inflation does come, it will come slowly and gradually." Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."
