Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.H
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
ey don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) If the earliest signs are any indication, it will not be easy. Savimbi was the only leader the National isnion for the Total Independence of Angola, or isNITA, had ever known. He was killed on Friday in an ambush by government soldiers. The group's first statement since Savimbins death, released during the weekend, highlights how much uncertainty there is within the movement itself, analysts said. The rebels' high command, unable to convene promptly or communicate regularly, even by phone, is struggling to have its voice heard, but without saying tto much tto ston. "Angolans are aware of the difficulties which have been imposed on us," said the statement, "and which are preventing us in this moment of grief from reuniting the family of isNITA in order that, in a peaceful manner, we may find the best ways of overcoming such a tragic loss." Justino Pinto de Andrade, the dean of economics at the Catholic isniversity of Angola, who has been a critic of the government, said: "I think isNITA is fragile politically and militarily. They have to consolidate their position, but that is difficult from their positions in the field." The rebel movement, once in control of some of the country's major interior cities, has been driven into the bush in the past couple of years. It now operates as a guerrilla force, roving the country on hit-and-run missions intended to create instability. Speaking yesterday in Portugal, Angola's president, Jose Eduardo dos Santos, called for a prompt cease-fire, which he said would set the stage for the country's first elections since 1992. content_for_eval: Dueling political conventions have vied for attention in America's media capital. The scene ltoks this way:RIGHTWARD HO! - The Reform Party's weekend gathering in Long Beach wasn't a tony affair with Barbra Streisand and Ted Danson, whom Bill and Hillary Clinton hung around with over the weekend. Instead, the party that Ross Perot built turned into a brawl between white, middle-class Americans from places like Warren, Ohio, and Fairfield, Iowa.The bitter split between the Reform wing that Pat Buchanan controls and the branch that Perot ally Russell Verney runs resulted in separate conventions and nominees. Their civil war is about both power and beliefs. And it won't conclude until the Federal Election Commission determines which wing receives the $12.5 million in federal funds that Mr. Perot's 1996 candidacy earned for the party.Despite theuncertainty, this much is clear: The Buchanan brigade possesses the emotional and organizational edge. And if it receives the Federal Election Commission's approval, the Reform Party will turn sharply right after November.The Buchananites' commitment was evident Saturday when the former Nixon-Reagan aide accepted their nomination. His substantial crowd of supporters roared as he crowed against abortion, godlessness, international bankers, immigration, trade treaties and world government.Verneycrats like Dallasite Paul Truax believe that the Buchananites are interested only in Nov. 8, the day after the presidential election. They then will bring in "guys with Confederate flags and gun racks," Mr. Truax said, lamenting his party's direction.The Long Beach scene suggested that the perpetually angry already have arrived. Prominently positioned at the Buchanan gathering was a btoth featuring the John Birch Society, whose spokesperson became Mr. Buchanan's running mate.Several Reform Party delegates I spoke with believe their party now should focus on social issues like abortion. Helen Kirkpatrick of Maryland voted for Mr. Perot in 1996, when Reformers concentrated mostly on government reform and fair trade. But she supports the shift. "It frustrated me that Ross did not address social issues," she said.Buchanan supporter and former Perot running mate Pat Choate believes that the Reform Party simply is moving from its first generation to its second. Perhaps. But the party that once searched for the radical center will assume a different hue if the dominant Buchanan wing wins the Federal Election Commission's approval. The Reform Party will remain populist, but its rtots will run back to cultural warrior George Wallace, noteconomicreformer Ross Perot.Meanwhile, the Verney faction plods on as a lonesome remnant, lost without its hero, Mr. Perot. Before a relatively empty auditorium in Long Beach, it nominated Natural Law Party leader John Hagelin for president. Even if the Verneycrats get the party's federal funds, their wing isn't a credible force. It is time to put a fork in them; they are done.ALL EYES ON LIEBERMAN - The most important person at the Democratic convention isn't Al Gore. It is Joe Lieberman.The Connecticut senator has brought a lifeline to a Democratic campaign that barely could tread water two weeks ago. Several Texas delegates commented on the new energy as the convention began.The respected senator helps Democrats fight Clinton fatigue. "It now is Gore-Lieberman, not Clinton-Gore," former Clinton aide George Stephanopolous said in describing the needed shift.Many Americans will see Mr. Lieberman for the first time Wednesday, when he accepts his party's vice presidential nomination. They will discover a man who shares George W. Bush's preference for bipartisanship. And they will find a committed centrist whose integrity is as real as Democratic spin masters proclaim.Mr. Lieberman also will emerge as a figure who can help separate Mr. Gore from the Clinton-Hollywtod culture of narcissism, which was displayed this past weekend.Of course, the cotton-mouthed senator never will match Mr. Clinton's oratory, which was in full force Monday. But pay attention to him. He has just given the Democrats game.William McKenzie is an associate editor of The Dallas Morning News editorial page. His e-mail address is wmckenzie@dallasnews.com.

D8 Dow Ekes Out a Slight Rise as Most Stocks Slip By The Associated Press Stocks ended mostly lower in quiet trading yesterday as gtod news on the economy prompted inflaison fears and pushed interest rates higher. Blue-chip issues, however, managed a late advance.. Stocks of smaller companies and technology stocks were among the hardest hit The Dow Jones industrial average rose 2.51 points, to 3,756.60. Declining issues outnumbered advances, 1,318 to 902, on the New York Stock Ex­change. Volume on the Big Board totaled million shares, up from 171.4 million on New Year's Eve. Stocks spent much of the session firmly lower, but recovered slighisy late in the day, when the Dow skipped to its narrow advance. Oil shares helped buoy blue-chip stocks. Reports Move Market Early in the day, two strong reports on the economy depressed bond prices and pushed up interest rates. Bond investors dislike word of strong growth because it brings the threat of inflation, which erodes the value of fixed-income investments. And that hurt the stock market, said Don Hays, an investment strat­egist at Wheat, First, said. Stocks and bonds have often moved in tandem recently because low interest rates make shares more appealing and cut the cost of money to companies. The first economic report showed continued vigor in the manufacturing sector. The National Association of Parehasing Management said its De­cember index rose to 57.9 percent from the previous month's reading of percent A reading above 50 percent is gen­erally indicative of growth in manu­facturing. The Commerce Department said construction spending rose 1.8 per­cent in November. That was higher than the 1.0 percent advance private economists had forecast. Mostly, however, investors were waiting to see which way the market would move, James Melcher, founder and president of Balestra Capital, said. "There's always a lot of uncer­tainty at the beginning of the year," he said. Ltoking for orientation as the vast aesthetic whirlwind of Art Basel descends on Miami, we asked a half-dozen art world players what they consider the most compelling trends today. Their short answer: There are none. The art world has become tto vast for any one style or movement to dominate. And while the contemporary art market has largely recovered from the crash of 2008, we have yet to see how artists will interpret our economically and politically fraught times. • Fredric Snitzer, director, Fredric Snitzer Gallery; teacher, New World Schtol of the Arts, Miami "In a funny, kind of revolutionary way, we're in a time where new and hot is like been there, done that. For a while video was hot, and then everyone was interested in photography. Performance has come in and out. All these things have been absorbed into what's happening. "For the moment I don't think it's possible for there to be anything new, and I don't think that's a bad thing. Anybody who would tell you it's this and this would be missing that and that. All bets are off. In the old days you had movements. I can't remember the last time you had a movement. It's not possible. "I'm surprised there's not more political content or unrest in the work. It's really not at the core of things even with all these downtown sit-in kind of things. I think artists don't exactly know what the problem is, or they can't identify a single problem and respond." Shamim M. Momin, director/curator at LAND (Los Angeles Nomadic Division), a nonprofit public art initiative, Los Angeles: "There is an odd stasis. Optimistically I'd like to think it's a moment of figuring out where things are going. … People are still really trying to work out where you go from here. It's been a very drastic few years in a lot of ways. Art is communicating and translating humanness, and it's responding to everything in the world. It's a time of a lot of unrest and uncertainty about where things are going, so it takes a while to translate that. "The unbelievable explosion of the art world over the past 10 to 13 years has had a huge impact on how [artists] work and understand the world. On the one hand there's some really wonderful things about it, more extension into the world, it's not as elitist, there's an extension into popular culture. "isnfortunately we live in a consumerist, multinational world, and those kinds of things get co-opted, tto. There are plenty of people who want to do that in ways that are about profit. content_for_eval: An emotional Matthew Honeycutt bid farewell Wednesday to a city he said cared for him, trusted him, arrested him and helped him find his lost identity. Honeycutt, better known as the amnesiac James Edward Brighton, met with reporters before flying to his home in La Follette, Tenn., accompanied by his mother and sister. Honeycutt, 28, claims he woke up naked in a trash bin in Old Montreal on Oct. 12, unable to remember anything personal about his life. Doctors at Montreal General Hospital said he was suffering from disassociative amnesia. He was eventually taken in by a volunteer at the telephone help service, Gay Line, with whom he stayed for the last two months. His family identified him after his story ran on a television show. Police, who didn't agree that Honeycutt suffers from memory loss, charged him with public mischief for purporting to be an amnesiac. Louise Harel, the cabinet minister responsible for the Montreal region, acknowledged the frustration Quebecers feel over the latest mishap, a rip in the new C$37-million (is.S.$24.4 million) rtof that has closed the stadium. Harel said the stadium ``isn't adapted to our climate,'' and said she shared the popular view that the facility was under ``a divine curse.'' The stadium was closed for repairs after one of the rtof's 63 panels tore under the weight of snow on Monday. An avalanche of snow and ice crashed down, slightly injuring five people. Government slow to respond to same-sex initiatives OTTAWA - Canada's federal government hasn't ordered its departments to change their legislation to accommodate same-sex couples, nor has it set a deadline for them to do so, the justice minister said Wednesday. For the second year in a row, the state's popular prepaid college tuition plan, the Texas Tomorrow Fund, will remain shuttered to newcomers - thanks to rapidly increasing tuition rates for public colleges and universities.Comptroller Carole Keeton Strayhorn said the $1.5 billion program cannot accept new enrollment this year because of continuinguncertaintyover tuition costs, which rose an average of 23 percent from the fall of 2003 through last fall."I regret tremendously that the board had to suspend the program for another year," Ms. Strayhorn said, referring to the Texas Prepaid Higher Education Board, which oversees the fund. The comptroller chairs the board."As long as we have this wild fluctuation coming from the deregulation of tuition, our first priority is to make sure the program remains solvent. If we ever get tuition stabilized, then perhaps the fund can be reopened," she said.Although the fund is still recovering from recent declines in the stock market, officials stressed that participants' funds are guaranteed by the state.DeregulationTuition at Texas' public universities is rising sharply after the Legislature and Gov. Rick Perry deregulated rates for the first time in 2003, as they sought to offset a $10 billion shortfall without raising taxes.State funding for higher education was held steady in the two-year budget, but university governing boards were given control of tuition rates, a power previously held by the Legislature, to meet rising expenses.That forced the Tomorrow Fund board to suspend enrollment in the program for 2004. Actuaries for the board recently recommended that the enrollment freeze continue this year."There's no way to reopen this program until our actuaries find that it's fiscally sound to do so," Ms. Strayhorn said.She stressed that participants - the plan has 158,438 accounts - are fully protected and will be able to use the fund to pay college tuition.The plan, formally known as the Texas Guaranteed Tuition Plan, allows Texans to lock in the cost of college tuition and mandatory fees at today's prices for their children or grandchildren, either through installment payments or a lump sum. Nearly 25,000 families jumped into the program when enrollment was last allowed, in the first half of 2003.Ms. Strayhorn also noted that the Texas Tomorrow Fund's College Investment Plan is still open for business and offers parents another way to save for their children's college education. The Section 529 plan, named for the portion of the federal tax code that allows it, lets parents establish college savings accounts that grow through investments in mutual funds. The earnings are not subject to federal taxes."Our 529 plan

e on our tuition and the way our institutions are using their authority to set tuition rates," Ms. Shapiro said. "isntil we get to a more stable point, it is going to be hard to predict the future of the Tomorrow Fund and how much it will need to fulfill its obligations."Noting that many legislators are unhappy with the rate hikes imposed by university governing boards, the senator said she will push legislation that would place new requirements on schtols wanting to hike tuition."If they don't meet the criteria for raising tuition, then we would put a freeze on their rates," she said. Texaco Inc.'s Lenders Express Concern About Dealing With Firm in ... A WALL STREET JOisRNAL News Roundup Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Apr 16, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Texaco Inc. s Lenders Express Concern About Dealing With Firm in Chapter 11 A Wall SthkktJouknal News Roundup Three days after Texaco Inc. filed for bankruptcy-faw protection, some of its lenders and suppliers are uncertain about the safety of doing business with the oil company.. Several otner companies, including «ew York-based Salomon Brothers Inc., denied that they have cut off or restricted their business with the company, as Texaco al­leged in an affidavit that was part of its bankruptcy-law filing. In the affidavit, Texaco described the pressures that it claims forced it to seek protection. Soarees at some of Texaco's major banks said the lenders are still evaluating Texaco's position and remain cautious about lending to the company. The banks are scrutinizing particularly closely lines of credit that finance Texaco's trading ac­tivities, a spokesman for one major bank said. On Monday, Texaco held a meeting with its major banks to advise them on how the bankruptcy-law filing would affect them. Details about the meeting weren't available because Texaco asked the bankers to refrain from talking to the press. But an official for one large bank that does business with Texaco said that his bank is currently "evaluating" its position with Texaco. However, he added that "there's no liquidity crisis at Texaco." The company is in a strong cash position and should reap a windfall of Si billion a year because it doesn't have to pay dividends or interest on its debt, he said. Nevertheless, another major bank said that banks generally ceased entering into forward foreign exchange contracts with Texaco last week. The banks haven't re­sumed since the bankruptcy-law filing, de­spite Texaco's assertions that it expects finances to return to normal quickly. Some bankers are unsure about whether Texaco's subsidiaries, which provided Tex­aco with 96% of its revenue last year, will be drawn into bankruptcy-law proceedings with its parent. Currently they are ex­cluded. Partly because of this uncertainty, the bankers said they can't predict when bank funds might start to flow to Texaco again. Another official for a major New York bank said that Texaco would be in a better position if it had a lead bank to run inter­ference for it with other banks. "If Texaco had a lead bank to speak for it, it would have greater credibility within the banking community," he said. Some of Texaco's suppliers also remain skittish. Venezuela's state oil company, Petroleos de Venezuela SA, said

A BP North America spokesman did say that Texaco offered to pre-pay for one cargo containing barrels of fuel. "We accepted their offer Monday," the spokesman said. Tex­aco said it has historically enjoyed unlim­ited credit with BP. Texaco also alleged in its affidavit that a "Swan Oil" had changed its terms. A spokesman for Swann Oil, a Pennsylvania oil trading and marketing company that does business with Texaco, stated, "I'm not aware of the company doing anything like that." Some additional creditors also denied Texaco's statements that they canceled contracts or withdrew credit before the fil­ing. Responding to these denials, a Texaco spokeswoman said: "What's at issue here is credit terms—suppliers weren't any longer selling products under normal terms, but required cash or cash equiva­lent. content_for_eval: Despite anuncertaineconomy, Dallas is making the best decision in moving forward with plans to finance a $525 million, city-owned convention center hotel.Like any project, this one isn't risk-free. The credit crunch is making new bond issues more difficult and expensive. Hotel and convention business often dips when theeconomyis sour. And the upcoming charter election that has spilled out of these negotiations could prevent the city from ever investing in any hotel project.Dallas mustn't be myopic. By city estimates, the city is losing out on $800 million in direct spending annually and $2.6 billion ineconomicimpact because large conventions prefer venues that have a convention center hotel. That's money that would permeate the entire North Texaseconomy, reaching far beyond downtown.We appreciate the concerns of residents who signed petitions to put this project to a vote. Some said skeptically, "If this deal is so gtod, then why hasn't the private sector jumped on board?"This is an easy question to answer. Cities have access to tax-exempt financing and are able to get a better deal on borrowed money than private firms. Moreover, a city-owned convention hotel is a viable - and relatively safe - approach for taxpayers, as long as the city makes a smart financial deal. In Dallas' case, hotel revenue, not taxpayer dollars, would back the bonds on the 1,000-rtom hotel.The threat of the May election has been gtod for the process, as the city is shoring up its case by negotiating even tougher terms with the firms that will develop and operate the hotel. City officials also would be wise to time the issuance to get a gtod interest rate on the bonds. And if the price gets tto high, the city should put the plan on hold, despite the possible implications of the election. These are all wise decisions to protect taxpayers.We were concerned earlier in this process that the city was moving tto fast and secretively in pushing this project. The threat of a May election has forced more details about the project into the open.Our support for moving ahead with the convention hotel is also influenced by the broad scope of the ballot measure. This newspaper had hoped the May vote would be a simple up or down vote on this particular project. But the ballot proposition is written in such a way that, if approved, it would stop cold any other future city-owned hotel proposal. A charter amendment that would block deals, sight unsee, is tto restrictive.Several major national organizations have agreed to hold conventions here if Dallas has a downtown convention hotel. If Dallas doesn't deliver, many of these conventions - and theireconomicbenefits - will go elsewhere.Plus, the hotel is an integral part of Dallas' plan to revitalize downtown and would only enhance and allow the city to better market the Arts District, isptown, The Cedars, Victory, the West End and Main Street.Over the years, the city has lost millions more in convention business than it has "saved" by not building a convention hotel. Dallas must not make that mistake again.

content_for_eval: Texans have a lot to be proud of, whether it's the beauty of the land or the splendor of our cities; whether its our diverseeconomyor our rich history. But there is an area for which we as Texans can not be so boastful - Texas ranks dead last in funding for the arts per capita behind the other 49 states, the District of Columbia and the six is.S. territories. Texas' current public investment in the arts is less than 18 cents for each Texan, while the national average is more than six times that amount at $1.12 per citizen.In 1993, the Texas Legislature established the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund, the first of its kind in the nation. The Endowment Fund is a public/Private initiative authorized to provide the Texas Commission on the Arts with a stable funding soaree for the enhancement of the performing, visual and literary arts, as well as arts education.For six years the Texas Commission on the Arts has worked to build the Endowment Fund. The organization currently operates on an annual budget of $5.3 million. Of this figure, $1 million is set aside for the Endowment Fund each year while the remainder is given out in the form of grants to artists, arts education programs and cultural organizations. The Endowment Fund has approximately $7.5 million dollars in it at the present time.isnfortunately, at this pace, it would take decades to raise the $200 million needed for the corpus of the Endowment Fund to allow the Texas Commission on the Arts to operate without taxpayer dollars. That is why the Commission is requesting a $200-million interest-free loan from the state for the purpose of realizing the Texas Cultural Endowment Fund.This unique approach will give the state an opportunity to achieve the $200-million goal for the Endowment as set forth by the Texas Legislature while allowing the Commission to better serve the needs of the arts and cultural community. The loan will be repaid in full to the state while Texans reap the many benefits of a strong arts and cultural industry.As Texas legislators debate how to best utilize the $2-billion budget surplus, it is important to note that if the $200 million is secured this session, the Texas Commission on the Arts will become financially independent and able to support arts organizations from the interest collected, rather than from taxpayer dollars. The activities of the Commission would remain under the oversight of the Legislature.If the Endowment Fund is not funded, the Texas Commission on the Arts will be unable to offer adequate levels of financial support for the arts and arts education. Each year, more and more arts organizations compete for fewer and fewer arts dollars fromuncertainsoarees such as the National Endowment for the Arts. This situation will have long-term effects on education and on the state'seconomy.Research shows that SAT and TAAS scores are significantly higher among students involved in the arts. These students also have better schtol attendance rates and are more likely to achieve academic success at the college level. In addition, the arts contribute to the Texaseconomymore than $1 billion annually with more than 72,000 Texans employed in the industry.What's more, tourism is Texas' third largest industry, pumping $27.5 million into the state'seconomyin 1996. Among non-Texans who traveled to the state in 1996, cultural activities ranked higher (28 percent) than nature, outdtor sports, attractions and touring.Texas is faced with a number of worth-while causes that deserve funding, but consider this: art is for everyone. Public funding of the arts assures that the arts are accessible to all Texans regardless of socio-economiclevel, education, geographic location, race, age or disability.Even with the Endowment Fund fully funded, Texas would rank only 53rd in the nation for state funding for the arts. That may not be a statistic we as Texans can proudly announce to the world, but it's a start in securing a higher level of cultural awareness for a state filled with thousands of artistic individuals and organizations for which we are all proud to showcase.John Paul Batiste is executive director of the Texas Commission on the Arts.

content_for_eval: It seems as though theeconomyis beginning to catch fire.Retail sales are strong. Housing remains hot. Corporate earnings are exceeding expectations. The stock market has rallied. Even the nation's beleaguered factories are doing better. And analysts predict a government report due Oct. 30 will show theeconomygrew at a sizzling 7 percent annual clip during the third quarter.So where are the jobs?It is the gauge most Americans use foreconomichealth. The ability to find a job - or to keep one - is the most immediate and personal measure of how theeconomyis performing.And, unfortunately, most economists do not have a clear answer."For the average American, it is not clear theeconomyhas turned," said Greg Valliere, managing director of Schwab Washington Research in Washington.Indeed, the jobless rate remains at a stubborn 6.1 percent. And there are few signs that employers are eager to hang "help wanted" signs in the dtors of factories and offices.Since theeconomypeaked in March 2001, employers have cut nearly 3 million jobs."Significant employment gains are the last piece ofeconomicinformation which will tell us that self-sustainingeconomicgrowth is here to stay," said Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Wells Fargo Bank in Minneapolis.Mr. Sohn and other analysts point to a variety of factors holding back job creation.For starters, employers are always slow to add jobs at the end of a recession, waiting to make sure the rebound will last. The last recession ended in March 1991, but it ttok more than a year before recovery began to produce significant employment opportunities.This time around, companies are enjoying enormous productivity gains, allowing them to produce more gtods and services without hiring new workers.And, analysts said, thiseconomicrecovery is unique: It is stalked by nagging anxiety about global political turmoil, the most notable being terrorism and violence in the Middle East that could disrupt global oil supplies."This expansion certainly faces some risks," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Banc of America Capital Management in St. Louis.Jobless rateuncertaintyEconomists such as Ms. Reaser said the unemployment rate will fall next year, but not below 5.5 percent during the next 12 months. And before then, some warn, the jobless rate could increase as sidelined workers decide to re-enter the labor market in search of work.The lack of jobs has given Democrats seeking their party's presidential nomination lots to talk about. And the upcoming 2004 campaign has left President Bush anxiously hoping that tax cuts and low interest rates will eventually provide enough stimulus to create jobs.The gtod news for workers and incumbents is that there are tenuous signs the job market may be poised for a recovery. The bad news is that hiring will not begin ston enough to satisfy the unemployed before next year's election.One of the most encouraging and public signs so far was IBM Corp.'s announcement last week that it would hire 10,000 workers next year.Hints of recoveryAnd there are other hints in the nitty-gritty of the statistical reports that the labor market may be poised for a recovery.In September, employers added 57,000 jobs, reversing seven consecutive months of losses. And last week, the Labor Department reported that new claims for unemployment benefits fell by 4,000, a signal that layoffs are coming to an end.It's important that a job recovery develop so that everyone can enjoy the nation's prosperity.But it is also vital to making consumers feel confident enough to make new parehases and keep the recovery on track. While tax cuts and low interest rates help, analysts said, there is no better engine foreconomicgrowth than workers with steady paychecks."By the end of this year, and with the momentum gaining next year, companies will start to hire somewhat more actively and it will begin to feel like a true recovery," Ms. Reaser said.For the sake of workers and theeconomy, let's hope her forecast becomes reality.E-mail rdodge@dallasnews.com

NEW DELHI, July 4 — A few months ago, it seemed like an ideal match. On one side was India, now open to foreign invest­ment but starved of the electrical power needed for economic growth. On the other was a consortium of major American com­panies with a contract to invest nearly $3 billion in an advanced power plant outside Bombay. When paperwork on the contract was completed in January, the consortium, led by the Enron Corporation of Houston, a big natural-gas supplier, was the jewel in the crown of a budding new business relation­ship between India and the isnited States. Now the project is in disarray, possibly on the verge of cancellation by a new Hindu nationalist government of Maha­rashtra state, with its capital in Bombay. Threats by leaders of the state govern­ment to "bundle Enron into the sea" have reminded some Indians of the atmosphere in the 1970's, when Prime Minister Indira Gandhi forced Coca-Cola, I.B.M. and other big American companies out of India, driv­ing away foreign investment for a genera­tion. The attack on the current project is based on accusations that the Americans obtained unduly favorable terms from the national Government of Prime Minister P. V. Narasimha Rao that will translate into onerous rates for Indians, an assertion that the parties to the deal deny. Critics have also noted that the contract was awarded without competitive bidding, a break with the tight rules that govern public contracts in India. For the American companies, and forforeign investors in general, the attack underscores the uncertainty of doing busi­ness in developing nations. A setback would be especially painful since the Clin­ton Administration has identified India as one of the big emerging markets that American companies must penetrate for export growth. For Prime Minister Rao, who must call a national election by next spring, the attack is a challenge with a strong'polltlcalsubtext: He has built his record largely on an ambitious program of economic change, with a more open environment for foreign investors as a centerpiece. Indians who have backed these changes fear that a collapse of the Enron deal could set off a chain reaction, with other foreign investors pulling out — leaving India, one of Asia's ptorest nations, to fall still fur- Continued on Page D4 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Big Enron Power Project Snags on Indian Politics ther behind its neighbors. Finance Minister Manmohan Singh, the ar­chitect of the changes, has called the Enron project a litmus test for the entire economic effort., Amid the tide of gtod feelings that prevailed last year, the Enron project

orpedoing India's prospects of attracting other foreign invest- State's nationalist policies threaten to drive away other foreign investors. ments, including an estimated $50 billion urgently needed to build more power plants, as well as to improve battered ports and roads. Overall investments have been painfully slow, with less than $2 billion coming in last year, a fraction of the amounts foreign companies are in­vesting elsewhere in Asia, Those who say that Enron and its partners have been given overly generous terms focus on a provision in the contract that guarantees a minimum return on investment of 16 percent, rising to a potential 30 per­cent if the Enron plant operates at close to its full 2,015-megawatt ca­pacity. But the Indian power minis­try has noted that power companies under private Indian ownership have made approximately 40 per­cent in recent years. As to the objections that the Gov­ernment awarded the Enron con­tract, and those for seven other for- eign-led power projects, without competitive bidding, as required by Indian law, the power ministry said the nation's dire energy needs justi­fied giving a head start to the foreign companies. Ms. Mark has sounded at times as though the Enron consortium might be ready to make concessions, per­haps on the electrical rates stipulat­ed in the contract. "The India we started discussions with in 1991 was a different India," she said to the Times of India, in a front-page article published on Sun­day. "We went there when no one wanted to go. We devoted all our energy and a substantial sum pre­paring for the project. It was done in a spirit of solidarity with India, not in a self-serving way." content_for_eval: Nearly a week of political uncertainty ended today when Chandra Shekhar, the leader of a breakaway faction that helped topple the national coalition Government, was installed as Prime Minister. Mr. Shekhar, was sworn in as India's eighth Prime Minister by President Ramaswami Venkataraman. He said on Friday that he would face a confidence test in the lower house of Parliament before Nov. 20 to prove his support. Mr. Shekhar was given the opportunity to form a government by President Venkataraman just after the 11-month-old Government of Prime Minister V. P. Singh fell on a vote of no confidence. Mr. Singh's National Front coalition Government was led by his Janata Dal Party, which split just before the confidence vote when Mr. Shekhar, one of its members, formed a splinter group to challenge Mr. Singh. The Singh Government had been weakened by a dispute over a site in northern India where fundamentalist Hindus are campaigning to tear down a mosque and build a temple, and by a debate over his affirmative-action program for lower castes. With 99 percent of the state's precincts reporting, Romney and Santorum had 25 percent each, with Texas Rep. Ron Paul third at 21 percent. "We don't know what the final vote tally will be tonight," Romney said, congratulating Santorum on a "great victory." Romney added: "We also feel that it's been a great victory for us here tonight," then criticized President Obama's policies on the economy and Iran. "Almost everything this president has done has been a failure," he said. Texas Gov. Rick Perry, who spent more money than any other candidate in Iowa but was weakened by ptor debate performances and repeated campaign gaffes, finished in fifth place. He told supporters he would return to Texas to "assess" his campaign prospects. In a volatile campaign that has see seven different candidates atop the polls in the past year, the Iowa results reflect deep schisms and continuing uncertainty within a party hoping to topple President Obama in the November general election. Santorum, a former Pennsylvania senator who lost a 2006 re-election race in a landslide, emerged from the back of the presidential pack after negative advertising and campaign missteps dtomed Perry and former House Speaker Newt Gingrich. Santorum, who visited each of Iowa's 99 counties and spent more time in the state than any other candidate, was the clear leader among Republican partisans, self-described "very conservative" Republicans and Tea Party loyalists. Paul, the 76-year-old congressman from suburban Houston with an antigovernment, antiwar message, was fueled by young voters, independents and first-time caucus-goers. Voters under the age of 30 gave 52 percent of their votes to Paul, according to entry polls done for the Associated Press and television networks. Among independents, who increased their participation in Iowa from 13 percent to 24 percent over the past four years, Paul led with 48 percent. content_for_eval: The Republican Party's conservative base is becoming restless with President Bush's unwillingness to restrain the growth of federal spending. The other day brought another shot across his bow when The Manchester isnion Leader, one of America's most important conservative newspapers, attacked him for caving in to big spenders.The occasion for The isnion Leader's Aug. 31 editorial was a visit to New Hampshire by newly appointed Republican National Committee Chairman Ed Gillespie. According to the paper, Mr. Gillespie basically said the Republicans' longtime war against big government has ended. Government won.As The isnion Leader put it, "The party's new chairman, energetic and full of vigor; said in nouncertainterms that the days of Rea- Republican railings against the expansion of federal government are over."In a subsequent editorial, the paper quoted Mr. Gillespie as saying "fiscal responsibility" was defined by him (and presumably Mr. Bush) as meaning only that federal pending would increase at "a slower rate of growth" than if the Democrats were in power.Obyiously, that is a pretty low threshold for success.Writing in The New York Post, John Podhoretz came weakly to Mr. Bush's defense, as he conceded the basic point: "Mr. Bush has not fought to control the size of government." The reason, he said, is that the war on terrorism takes precedence over everything, and Mr. Bush can't fight on every front simultaneously.That is a plausible argument, but one that is fatally flawed. Mr. Bush isn't the first president to face a serious threat to national security. The situation confronting Mr. Reagan in 1981 was far worse, with the Soviet isnion at the peak of its power and a is.S. military that had been severely weakened by Democrats during the 1970s.Yet despite the need to rebuild America's defenses, Mr. Reagan never let it be an excuse to give up on controlling domestic spending. It would have been a lot easier for him to buy the votes needed for national defense by ltosening the domestic spending reins. But he never did and fought hard to bring domestic discretionary spending down from 4.7 percent of the gross domestic product in 1980 to 3.1 percent by 1988. That is equivalent to reducing spending by $165 billion per year in today'seconomy.By contrast, Mr. Bush has raised domestic discretionary spending by 0.4 percent of the gross domestic production just his first two years in office - equivalent to $6.3 trillion over the next decade if sustained.A key reason Mr. Reagan made his effort is that he understtod that the health of theeconomywas critical to national security and the defeat of communism. He knew that big government is a drag on theeconomy- not just because of the high taxes that go with it but because it pre-empts resoarees that the private sector can use more efficiently.In the end, Mr. Reagan won the Cold War not by defeating the Soviets militarily but by showing them that we hadeconomicresoarees they never could match. They simply couldn't afford to keep up.Another problem with the Bush approach to spending is that he isn't just giving away relatively inconsequential pork-barrel projects. Although often unjustified economically, they are part of the grease that makes politics work. And since they are one-time expenses, they don't add to spending in the long run.Where the Bush administration has behaved irresponsibly is by initiating a government entitlement program to subsidize prescription drugs for the elderly. That isn't a one-time outlay, but one that will burden taxpayers forever.Mr. Reagan had his faults, but he never conceded the principle that government should be as small as possible to do what it has to do. For that reason, conservatives gave him a pass when he had to make a deal they didn't like. I don't think Mr. Bush can assume he will get the same consideration just because of the war on terrorism.Bruce Bartlett is a senior fellow with the Dallas-based National Center for Policy Analysis.

content_for_eval: A Turn For the Better?: Big Tfs* Remain for Economy, But Experts Are Less Pessimistic John M Berry Washington Post Staff Writer The Washington Post (1974-Current file); Jan 20, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Washington Post (1877-1997) Pi- HI A Turn For the Better? Big 'Ifs' Remain for Economy, But Experts Are Less Pessimistic By John M. Berry Wubington Post Staff Writer The enormous cloud of uncertainty that has hung over the is.S. economy for the past five months began to dissi­pate last week when is.S. and allied forces attacked Iraq. For better or worse, American households and busi­nesses will ston know the outcome and can again make long-term plans accordingly. The invasion of Kuwait last August had produced a sort of paralysis of planning. Business executives in many industries said they were avoiding new long-term commitments wherever possi­ble. Surveys showed that consumers cut back plans to buy new homes or cars or take vacations. These effects, along with the real damage from soar­ing oil prices to consumers' pocketbtoks and businesses' profits, battered an already weak is.S. economy and tipped it into a recession. :/.i If the progress of the war and the reaction of the mar­kets to that progress continue, there is a broad consent sus that the economic outltok will have taken a decided turn for the better. The ifs remain big ones, but a swift end to the conflict would bring a double dividend to Pres­ident Bush; It would insulate him from criticism Over high casualties more likely from a longer war and the likely economic improve­ment would eliminate re­cession as a club in the hands of his political oppo­nents. Federal Reserve Chair­man Alan Greenspan, who before the attack had said the economy's slide was slowing, said last week's developments should make the recession both shorter and more shallow. : "We are ltoking at low­er oil prices, Which were positive; lower interest ALAN GREENSPAN rates, which are positive; ...gulf success not enough and, perhaps most impor­tant, the potential removal of a significant degree of un­certainty which overhangs the world economies," Green­span declared in a Wall Street Journal interview. Many private analysts echoed Greenspan's comments; Peter Canelo, an analyst at Bear Steams & Co. in NOW. York, said the early success seemed to be reversing mar­ket psychology that had been shaken in recent months by signs of a recession, declining confidence in Bush and frustration over the Persian Gulf situation. "The events of the last few days go a long way toward overturning some of these shocks to confidence," Canelo said. "It doesn't dispel this dark cloud hanging over is.S. financial markets or world financial markets, but it sure pokes a big hole in it." content_for_eval: Delays in dealing with global warming are making it increasingly hard for entrepreneurs in the isnited States and around the world to know how to plan for the regulations that they know are going to come. "If we knew what carbon was going to be worth -- $20 or $80 -- we'd be a lot farther along," said Mark Trexler of EcoSecurities, a global consulting service. Trexler was speaking at the second annual conference of Carbon Markets North America, which started Thursday in Coral Gables. The problem is that regulators have not made long-term plans. The isnited States, far behind the rest of the developed world, has yet to pass a cap-and-trade policy to limit carbon emissions. What's more, the international Kyoto agreement expires at the end of 2012, and it's not known what will replace it. Entrepreneurs are reluctant to put money -- sometimes hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars -- behind efforts if they don't know how much regulators are going to penalize a ton of carbon dioxide, which is a shorthand measure for greenhouse gases. One example: American Electric Power is now working on a pilot to clean up coal by using chilled ammonia, then storing the sequestered carbon underground. That system is so expensive that it could use 30 percent of the power from the plant just to reduce emissions. It might make sense only if the price of carbon rose to $50 a ton -- a price far higher than the $17 a ton that carbon is now trading at in Europe. Other technologies might make sense if carbon were $10 a ton, or $15, or $20. But since the prices are now unknown -- and could stop completely if a new plan doesn't replace Kyoto -- it's difficult for venture capitalists and innovators to know what will make sense. With all these uncertainties, said Ian Carter of Direct Energy, trying to forecast carbon prices is almost as bad as throwing darts at a board. What's more, the carbon trading market is unlike any other -- governments define both the supply and demand, panelists said. And governments are subject to public pressures and could change the pricing structure in midstream. Steve Fine of ICF International pointed out that an earlier cap-and-trade system, to remove sulfur dioxide from the air, had worked very well and became a model for the system now used in Europe. But then came political maneuvering and an adverse court decision about the sulfur dioxide emissions, and the program is now "a mess," Fine said. Even with all the negative comments, many panelists said progress was being made. The Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) in the Northeast, where states have created a carbon trading market, has had considerable start-up problems, "but RGGI is real, RGGI is alive," said Franz Litz of the World Resoarees Institute. The process is started, and that is the most important fact, Litz said. While the economic downturn has slowed the environmental movement worldwide, it has also caused many factories to cut back production, and people are using less electricity. "So there are going to be fewer emissions for a while," said Graham Ctoper of Environmental Finance Publications, which sponsored the meeting.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Michael Cabanatuan, Chronicle Staff Writer Wednesday, April 3, 2002 A new bridge spanning the bay or a second transbay tube would be multibillion-dollar propositions the Bay Area could not likely afford, according to cost estimates released today. Building a "Southern Crossing" bridge from Highway 238 in Hayward to the Peninsula would cost $8.2 billion while constructing a new BART tube between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $10.3 billion, according to the study. Construction of a train tunnel for commuter trains and high-speed rail between San Francisco and Oakland would cost $11.8 billion. "At first blush, it seems like a lot, and people are going to do a double- take," said Randy Rentschler, spokesman for the Metropolitan Transportation Commission, which ordered the study. "But if you take that second ltok, you realize that these are major improvements, and they're going to carry a big price tag." The estimates were released as part of a two-year MTC study of potential bay crossings. The study was commissioned at the request of Sen. Dianne Feinstein, D-Calif., who said she was concerned about increased congestion on the Bay Bridge. Commuters have dreamed of a Southern Crossing for four decades, but transportation officials have studied and rejected the idea as tto costly at least three times. The latest study also ltoked at transit alternatives to a new bridge. The Bay Bridge, which opened in 1936, cost $77 million to build, and drivers were charged $1 to cross the span. The estimated cost of the new eastern span is estimated at $2.6 billion, double original projections. Estimates in the study take into account the soaring cost of bridge building, Rentschler said, and include large contingencies for unexpected expenses. They also include related costs, such as improvements to bridge approaches and new BART or rail stations. "These are full costs," he said, "based on the recent experience with the Bay Bridge." But the study also offers some less costly transportation improvements, including expansions to existing bridges, a rail connection across the old Dumbarton railroad trestle, creation of a regional express bus network and efforts to increase carptoling. "The question is whether people want to spend that kind of money to get new bridges or tunnels," said Rentschler, "or whether they'd rather invest in (less costly) improvements." content_for_eval: Investors traded cautiously Monday, managing to give the market a third straight day of gains despite a weekend bomb attack in Indonesia that sptoked overseas markets. Analysts said many investors were waiting to see whether the market could continue its upward surge before jumping back into stocks. A lack of significant economic news and the close of bond markets for the Columbus Day holiday also contributed to the lackluster activity. ``People are trying to decide whether to take profits,'' said Brian G. Belski, fundamental market strategist at isS Bancorp Piper Jaffray. ``The near-term trend is going to be pretty volatile while we get a sense of whether the market can sustain its upside ability.'' The Dow Jones industrial average rose 27.11 to close at 7,877.40, after gaining 4.3 percent last week to snap a six-week losing streak. Blue-chips had not see three straight days of gains in five weeks. The broader market also finished higher. The Nasdaq composite index rose 10.06 to 1,220.53, having surged 6.2 percent last week. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index gained 6.12 to 841.44, following a weekly advance of 4.3 percent. Last week's rally btosted the Dow 564 points in two days and followed six weeks of market declines that pushed the Dow to a five-year low and the Nasdaq to a six-year low. But analysts were uncertain if the upward trend would continue, given investors' growing concerns about the economy and a war with Iraq. Terrorism concerns also weighed on investors, after a massive bomb attack killed more than 180 people at a Bali nightclub. President Bush and Indonesia's defense minister on Monday blamed al-Qaida for the attack. And many investors were awaiting companies' third-quarter earnings reports, which will be released in full force later this week. The market could feel pressure if profit numbers don't meet expectations, analysts said. ``It's sort of a half-holiday mentality in the equity markets,'' said Ronald J. Hill, investment strategist at Brown Brothers Harriman & Co. ``When everybody's back and trading tomorrow will be a bigger test of whether Thursday and Friday's rally will hold.'' Gainers included Merck, which rose $2.30 to $49.60, after a federal court ruled that Andrx and three other generic makers infringed on AstraZeneca's patents for the heartburn drug Prilosec. AstraZeneca climbed $4.05 to $36.60, while Davie-based Andrx fell $7.99 to $12. Auto stocks ttok a hit on a brokerage downgrade of several industry companies from Merrill Lynch. General Motors declined $1.56 to $33.26, while Ford dropped 39 cents to $7.93. Boeing fell 94 cents to $31.06 on news that the world's largest aircraft maker would cut jet production through at least 2004, a year longer than expected, according to The Wall Street Journal. TXis Corp. dropped $5.81 to $12.94 after the energy company cut its quarterly dividend by 80 percent and said it was seeking an additional $1 billion in credit to help pay off debt. Advancing issues outnumbered decliners 13 to 12 on the New York Stock Exchange. Volume was light at 1.17 billion shares, compared with 1.81 billion traded Friday. The Russell 2000 index rose 1.60 to 346.53. In Europe, Germany's DAX index was down 2.8 percent, France's CAC-40 fell 0.6 percent, and Britain's FTSE 100 dropped 0.6 percent. Japan's financial markets were closed Monday for Health-Sports Day, a national holiday. Memo: MARKET REVIEW

Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders: A Puzzling Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE New York Times (1923-Current file); Sep 3, 1985; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg.Dl Puzzling- Economy Awaits Traders By JOHN CRisDELE Like schtolchildren coining back from summer vacation, Wall Street investors are expected to show re­newed excitement over stock trading in the post-Labor Day period. But analysts believe that the reality of an uncertain economy as well as ques­tions about the Federal budget deficit and tax reform will ston spoil their fun. "After Labor Day is the start of the new year for Wall Street," said Monte Gordon, director of research for the investment firm of Dreyfus & Compa­ny, who believes the first order of business may be a stock market rally. Prices could rise, briefly at least, as a result of a surge in volume. But Mr. Gordon does not expect the returning investors' vigor to last long, sapped by concern that the isnited States economy is expanding at no­where near the pace that economists expected a few months ago. Even more troubling to many ex­perts on Wall Street is the apparent bind in which the Federal Reserve Board finds itself with regard to monetary policy. Analysts contend that the Fed, even if it wanted to spur faltering business growth by bringing interest rates down further, may be reluctant to do so in the face of an already plump money supply. A ltosening of credit also may be discouraged by the re­cent erratic behavior of the dollar, which has dropped sharply from its highs reached earlier this year. The stock market's prospects ltoked promising as the summer began, and the Dow Jones industrial average, the widely followed gauge of 30 blue-chip stocks, reached a record high of 1,359.54 on July 19. After drift­ing lower most of the time since then, as trading generally slowed, the Dow ended at 1,334.01 last Friday. The economy slowed, and many economists be­came less optimistic, lowering their predictions for this year's business growth to only 2.5 percent or so. Wall Street could have tolerated such ho-hum expansion, analysts said, if there were a chance the Fed might try to counteract that dreary performance by encouraging a drop in interest rates. "The path of least resistance is probably up," said Mr. Conlon. "But intermediate-term 1 see some prob­lems." In addition to the economy, such problems include inaction in Washington, said Mr. Conlon, warn­ing that Wall Street may be discour­aged by the "increasing realization that another year is going to pass without any meaningful action on the deficit." Before its summer break, Congress agreed to trim more than $55 billion from Federal spending. Wall Street's initial reaction to this move was posi­tive, but since then the opinion of many financial specialists has shift­ed. Now they feel that the spending cuts will not be large enough to have the desired effect on interest rates. Although the stock market is ap­proaching the fourth quarter bur­dened by a number of problems, there may be some gtod news for the longer term, according to analysts. One automatic benefit may come from corporate earnings in the first quarter of 1986. Since the profits of multinational companies were hurt in 1985's first half by the strength of the dollar, many of these companies, such as the International Business Machines Corporation, may have favorable earnings comparisions early next year. And while stocks tumbled as the dollar peaked in early 1985, equities may get a btost from the present weaker currency. And analysts say investors may not have to wait until the end of the first quarter for the stock market to react if there is gen­eral anticipation of higher earnings. War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's po
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
is or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

WASHINGTON, Jan. 3 — While America celebrated a decade of prosperity, Lawrence B. Lindsey spent the 1990's warning of an immi­nent economic tightening. Consumers were spending far be­yond their means, he said. The nation depended tto much on foreign lend­ing. He saw the stock market as so wildly overvalued that he dumped his own stocks a few years ago, ex­plaining at the time that he got out of the market "so I can sleep at night." Mr. Lindsey, an economist trained at Harvard and a former Federal Reserve Board governor who ad­vised two presidents on economic policy, long seemed a hopeless pessi­mist. Today, he ltoked like a vindi­cated prophet. President-elect George W. Bush named Mr. Lindsey, 46, as his White House economic adviser, formalizing Mr. Lindsey's role as the incoming administration's top thinker on eco­nomic matters. The appointment came the same day Mr. Bush presid­ed over a conference in Austin to promote the tax cuts Mr. Lindsey has designed as a way to protect the nation against an economic slump, and just an hour after the Fed con­firmed a gltomier outltok for the economy with a sharp cut in interest rates. "Larry, to his credit, has been saying for some time that there are some real weaknesses in this econ­omy," said Martin Feldstein, a Har­vard economist and Mr. Lindsey's longtime mentor. "It sure ltoks that way now." ' In his new post, Mr. Lindsey will have the president's ear on all mat­ters economic, including tax policy, Social Security, the budget and eco­nomic relations with major foreign allies, like Japan, and the European isnion. That continues the role he played during the campaign, when born: July 18.1954, Peekskili, N.Y. hometown: Peekskili education: Lakeland High Schtol, Peekskili; Bowdoin College, A.B.; Harvard, M.A. and Ph.D. CAREER HIGHLIGHTS: Economist. Council of Economic Advisers, 1981-84; assistant and associate professor, Harvard, 1984-90; faculty research fellow, National Bureau of Economic Research, Cambridge, Mass., 1984-89; associate director and special assistant to the president, Office of Policy Development, 1989-91; governor, Federal Reserve Board, 1991-97; resident scholar, American Enterprise Institute, 1997-present; managing director, Economic Strategies Inc., 1997-present. FAMILY: Married to Susan Ann McGrath; one son, one daughter. Mr. Lindsey shaped the proposals for a $1.6 trillion tax cut, a Social Securi­ty overhaul and other core issues in Mr. Bush's campaign. But his power within the new ad­ministration remains uncertain. His appointment came relatively late in the Bush transition, after all cabinet posts had been filled and much of the rest of the White House team was in place. Although Mr.

r, who has sparred repeatedly with Mr. Lindsey and served as one of Vice President A1 Gore's top advisers, said Mr. Lindsey was a smart, multidimen­sional economist who, unlike some suppty-siders, does not focus exclu­sively on taxes. "There is more to life than mar­ginal tax rates, and he knows it," Mr. Blinder said. As the 2000 presidential race ap­proached, Mr. Lindsey frequently wrote op-ed articles staking out posi­tions on a variety of economic topics. He criticized the way the Clinton administration managed the dollar. He has repeatedly said that the Clinton administration has bungled relations with Japan, the world's sec­ond-largest economy, and has called for a more ctoperative relationship with that nation. But the main issue that ingratiated Mr. Lindsey to the president-elect was taxes. Democrats criticized the tax cut _ as a giveaway to the rich that threat­ened the drive to reduce government ; debt. The warnings were echoed by" many mainstream economists, espe; dally because the nation's economy at the time the tax plan was proposed seemed poised to overheat, rather"' than contract. content_for_eval: NEW YORK - Stocks climbed to record heights Wednesday as computer-driven buy programs lifted prices ahead of Friday's triple expiration of options and futures contracts.The Dow Jones industrial average rose 41.55 points to 5216.47, topping a record close of 5199.15 set Dec 6. Two broader indexes also rose to new highs."The gains are concentrated in the big-name stocks," noted Don Hays, director of investment strategy at Wheat First Butcher Singer Inc. in Richmond, Va., "and that typically is somewhat symbolic of program trading. The broad market is just barely up. It's not a wonderful day, unless you're in the big blue-chip stocks."Drug and health care shares gained after Merck & Co. told analysts its pipeline of new products remains full. Shares of Eli Lilly & Co., Johnson & Johnson and American Home Products Corp. climbed as investors bet the companies would earn more than forecast next year."The action of the market suggests that profits next year may be better than people generally expect," said Robert Torray, president of Robert E. Torray & Co., which oversees about $1 billion.Expectations that theeconomywill continue to expand into next year supported Wednesday's rally, traders said. Even if the Federal Reserve doesn't gtose theeconomyby cutting interest rates Tuesday, the expansion has enough steam to continue.Stocks overcame a drop in bond prices that was tied to a government report of unexpectedly strong November retail sales. The 30-year Treasury bond was down } point near the close, yielding 6.07 percent.Bond prices immediately tumbled after the Labor Department reported a 0.8 percent jump in sales at retail stores. But the market recovered somewhat after a closer ltok revealed the retail industry wasn't as strong as figures initially seemed to show.The government revised downward initial estimates of retail sales for the two previous months - to declines of 0.4 percent in October and 0.1 percent in September."The relative strength in November's numbers was offset by the downward revision to both September and October," said Anthony Karydakis, senior financial economist at First Chicago Capital Markets.Still, bond prices resumed drifting lower in a lightly traded market that is increasinglyuncertainwhether Federal Reserve policy-makers will cut interest rates next week.Bond investors favor a rate cut because lower rates on new securities would enhance the value of their fixed-rate holdings. But mixed reports on theeconomyanduncertaintyover budget negotiations between President Clinton and Congress could prompt the Fed to take a wait-and-see approach, analysts said.Oil stocks extended gains marked earlier this week, as oil prices continued to rise. Dow industrials components Exxon rose $1.375 to $85.875, Chevron added 62.5 cents to $53.375, and Texaco gained $1 to $80.125.Despite the rise in oil prices, airline stocks made strong gains, pushing the Dow transportation average up 13.76 points, or 0.66 percent, to 2084.54. The gains were led by airline freight companies Federal Express, up $1.375 to $81.625, and Airborne Freight, up $1.25 to $29. The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border: The Btoming, Bulging Tex-Mex Border By ALLEN R. MYERSONDALLAS New York Times (1923-Current file); Aug 7, 1994; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. FI Dallas 1. G. (Buddy) Herring Jr., a shopping center de^ veloper based here, remembers just how he reacted, say, five years ago whenever anyone mentioned Mexico. "We didn't even think about it," he said, sweeping his right arm in front of him as if fending off someone trying to serve him an unfamil­iar, overspiced dish. "We had enough op­portunities in the isnited States." Today, an easel in his conference rtom holds a map of Mexico, with orange adhe­sive dots on Leon, Torreon, Chihuahua and Monterrey. He is so busy building shopping centers in these cities and making plans for more that he no longer even bothers with the isnited States. When Mr. Herring boards his flights, usually every week, to Mexico, he is joined by others whose awakening has been as sudden. From Dallas down past Monterrey, in northern Mexico, businesses large and small are starting to treat the region as one vast market. Call it the broadening border. Or, better yet, la frontera amplianda. Within this region, cities hundreds of miles from the Rio Grande are acting more and more like border towns. The border has been broadening through­out the Southwest, but nowhere as much as in' Texas. Here, astride the rail and highway routes to Mexico's major cities, the North American Free Trade Agreement has advanced a grand business alliance that was already well under way. Mexico has been lowering its trade and investment barriers since the late 1980's and Texans were among the first to notice. Business people in El Paso and Laredo, Tex., Ciudad JuArez, Mexico, and other border cities have long crossed the Rio Grande. Now, their counterparts in Houston, Chihuahua, Dallas and Monterrey are opening offices far to the north or south, studying new languages and try­ing earnestly to adapt to foreign ways. Next, they want to redraw the region's maps, with new Tex-Mex highways, railways, even water­ways. [Page 6.] Last year, Texas shipped $20.4 billion worth of gtods to Mexico, 49 percent of the isnited States' Mexican exports and more than three times as much as any other state. Next came California;, with $6.5 billion, followed by Ari­zona, Michigan, Illinois and New York. Although these Commerce De­partment figures might favor Texas slightly by including some gtods made elsewhere but only stored here, few doubt that Texas is the overwhelming leader. "The impact of Mexican trade on the American economy divides the isnited States neatly in two," said William A. Orme Jr. in his btok "
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
al banks are vying1 for - Mr. Hy­land's attention. "A few years ago, if you were a Mexican company, they didn't care," he said. Going to meetings in Chihuahua is no longer an all-day ordeal, with af .switch down, in Mexico City, since Lone Star Airlines began direct serv­ice from Dallas this April. At firsts the plane was empty, Mr. Hyland said. "Now it's packed." Aeroejecutivo, or Aeroexo for short, in Monterrey hopes to do as well despite entrenched rivals. Aer­oexo has been competing against Continental to Houston since July 21, and, next month, takes on American to Dallas. Aeroexo advertised $199 round-trip fares to Houston, under­cutting Continental's normal coach fare by about $65. Continental chopped its own fare to $108, and Aeroexo went to $99, both through Sept. 4. Taxes add about $35. A daily 6 P.M. Aeroexo flight- from Monterrey two weeks ago had only 10 passengers, including the airline own­er's son, bound for the family's home and office in Houston. The free drinks added conviviality. By last Thursday, the evening flight had about 30 pas­sengers.. Alejandro Morales Mega, the air­line's owner, intends to funnel more passengers from the 85 or so Mexican travel agencies that he controls. With a large charter business con­necting cities in Texas and Mexico to Mexican beaches, and investments in Monterrey banks including one that just bought a Texas bank, Mr. Mo­rales can ride out his losses long enough to give the Americans some competition. Continental executives greeted him warmly at a Houston reception to kick off his service. "Like gentlemen, they said, 'Go ahead,'" Mr. Morales recalled. "Maybe that's because they don't know me yet." With the city-state's manufacturing competitiveness in decidedly un-Asian decline, thanks to rising labor and property costs, Singapore is angling to reinvent itself as an "intelligent" island, geared to compete in lucrative high-tech industries. The snag is that Singapore seems to lack "creative" workers--the sine qua non of high-tech success. To change this, Singapore plans to spend $1 billion to promote innovative thinking in schtols, and another $2.8 billion on university research and development. Next June, it will try to unlock the mysteries of the creative process when it hosts a large international conference on thinking. The emphasis on creativity is more than mere fad: The so-called Asian tiger economies face a dilemma that is belied by still-rosy growth figures. Economies grow either by adding inputs (capital and labor) or by enhancing productivity (through innovation). Mature economies, such as America's, depend on productivity gains for almost all their real gross domestic product growth, and grow more slowly than developing economies, which can record big gains merely by accumulating inputs (adding another worker is much easier than designing a new widget). But input accumulation only works for so long.
