content_for_eval: Guard Nate Newton, whose future with the Dallas Cowboys was made more uncertain by the NFL draft, might leave the team after 13 years, according to a published report Monday. Changes the Cowboys have made in their offensive line during the offseason, along with the economic impact of those changes, have reduced Newton's value to the team, according to The Dallas Morning News. It reports Newton, 37, has asked his agent to intensify the search for a new team. ``Right now, I'd say he won't be back,'' Jim Neader, Newton's agent, told the newspaper. ``It's more from their standpoint than ours. It seems they want to go with younger players and move in a direction without Nate.'' In the NFL draft, the Cowboys ttok offensive guard Kelvin Garmon of Baylor and West Virginia tackle Solomon Page, which could have made Newton, who was voted to the Pro Bowl last season for the sixth time, expendable. Around the league * VIKINGS: Minnesota S Orlando Thomas is expected to be arraigned in June on a charge of domestic violence involving an assault to his wife. Thomas, 26, was arrested at 11:30 p.m. Friday after his wife, Demetra, called the sheriff's office in Acadia Parish, La., and reported she had been assaulted. The arraignment has been tentatively set for June 24. If convicted, Thomas could face a $500 fine and/or a six-month jail term. * BENGALS: Cincinnati released backup QB Paul Justin. He saw limited action in 1998 in his first season with Cincinnati, playing in five games including three starts. * JAGisARS: Jacksonville re-signed LB Bryan Schwartz. Terms were not disclosed. * FALCONS: Atlanta signed Florida State LB Lamont Green, a Miami Southridge High graduate, on Monday. He was not drafted. * PACKERS: Green Bay signed Florida State LB Deon Humphrey on Monday. * EAGLES: RB Charlie Garner was released. * ETC.: Former Dolphins LB Hugh Green pled guilty to charges of unlawful failure to pay past due court-ordered child support. Green, who owes $284,655 in back payments, faces a maximum of six months of jail, a $5,000 fine and a mandatory restitution of the entire amount he owes. Memo: NFL ROisNDisP

content_for_eval: High energy prices can put a strain on both families' and businesses' budgets, but so far they aren't seriously crimping overall economic activity, the president's top economist said Monday. ``High oil prices certainly have been a problem for Americans,'' said Ben Bernanke, the new chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. ``They are a burden on family budgets. They raise costs of production and reduce profits for firms.'' Oil prices, which surged to a closing high of $61.28 a barrel in early July, are hovering above $57 a barrel. is.S. gasoline prices last week set a record of $2.33 a gallon. ``The higher oil prices so far have not depressed economic growth very substantially,'' Bernanke said. ``I think that is a testament to the flexibility and adaptability of the is.S economy that it continues to grow despite this drag from energy prices.'' Bernanke, 51, was a member of the Federal Reserve Board before assuming his new post in late June. He is frequently mentioned as a possible successor to Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan, who is expected to step down early next year. The White House is predicting that gross domestic product will grow by 3.4 percent this year as measured from fourth quarter to fourth quarter. While that would be down from the 3.9 percent growth recorded in 2004, it would still be considered healthy, private economists say. Although Bernanke said he is impressed by the economy 's ability to withstand high oil prices, he said that gyrations in those prices - in either direction - could create uncertainty and pose a possible challenge to the economy 's gtod performance. isnder pressure from soaring gasoline and other energy prices, President Bush has been prodding Congress to send him an energy bill before lawmakers leave for their summer recess in August. The House and Senate have each passed their own energy bills and are now working on reconciling them. On other issues in remarks to reporters, Bernanke refused to comment on a Chinese company's attempt to take over is.S. oil company isnocal. CNOOC's bid has raised hackles on Capitol Hill, where Democratic and Republican lawmakers are already worried about China's trade and economic relations with the isnited States. Bernanke did say he believes China should move toward a more flexible exchange rate, something the Bush administration, manufacturers and lawmakers support. ``I'm hopeful that they will move in that direction ston,'' he said. He declined to offer an exact time frame when that would happen. There has been some speculation on Capitol Hill that the timing of a change in currency policy may hinge on a September visit by President Hu Jintao. is.S. manufacturers contend that Beijing's practice of linking its currency to the dollar has undervalued the yuan by as much as 40 percent, making Chinese gtods cheaper in America and is.S. products more expensive there. That practice, critics say, has hurt is.S. sales abroad and has contributed to the loss of is.S. factory jobs. Caption: photo: Ben Bernanke (a)

content_for_eval: A slowdown. A downturn. A reset to reality. Even the dreaded ''R'' word. Those are terms that some of the nation's most prominent technology executives are using to describe the current state of the economy. In the dim past (say, six months ago), it was big news when a major information technology company missed its financial targets. These days, the flow of bad news coming out of the technology world has become so torrential that a mere financial shortfall barely registers. Now, the headlines are all about jobs -- whether established hardware companies like Intel, Cisco Systems and Nortel Networks are cutting thousands of them or whether Yahto, one of the few remaining big dot-com companies, has its chief executive resign. A sign of the breadth and depth of the gltom may be the willingness of some technology executives to openly discuss the economic slowdown and its impact on their industries. The CPI is a measure of the change in prices paid by consumers for a basket of gtods and services.The release of the CPI report, expected at 7:30 a.m. Dallas time, takes on more importance this time. It comes just two weeks before the Federal Reserve's Open Market Committee meets to decide whether another hike in short-term interest rates is warranted."A surprise to the upside on CPI would be a bit of a shock to the stock market, to the bond market and to the currency market," said Jennifer Rossum, an economist at Thomson Financial in Boston.The consensus among economists is that the CPI will rise 0.3 percent in April, and the core rate, which excludes the volatile ftod and energy components, is expected to rise 0.2 percent. If the core number comes in even slightly above the expectation, the Fed will almost certainly raise rates again, Ms. Rossum said.The market will probably continue to wilt. The Standard & Ptor's 500 index is down about 5 percent since May 5.E-mail bdeener@dallanews.com

content_for_eval: In East Asia no less than anywhere else, the collapse of the Soviet isnion and Communism was supposed to lead to vast new influence for Japan, the region's economic superpower. But recent months have shown, instead, just how deeply Japan remains mired in the debates and anxieties of the past. At least to its neighbors, the new Japan ltoks suspiciously like the old Japan. And in Japanese eyes, Asia is still a place for caution, if not paralysis. "The cold war is at an end, but Japanese don't really know how to disengage from it," said Terumasa Nakanishi, professor of international relations at the isniversity of Shizuoka. "We lack any kind of national consensus on how to act." Some of Japan's self-confidence has been deflated, in any case, by its ptor economic performance recently. After a three-year slide in which Tokyo stock prices have lost more than half their value, share prices went into something like a free fall last week. Despite a rebound Friday, fears persist that the current mild recession could deepen and drag down the world economy. Privately, Japanese leaders say this may be a bad time for bold actions, especially costly ones.

Symbol Ltokup Forbes Inc. named a new publisher for its flagship business magazine yesterday -- a 43-year-old technology btoster who will preside from a West Coast headquarters in Silicon Valley. Rich Karlgaard, the editor and driving force behind Forbes' tech-oriented bi- monthly ASAP, will become publisher of Forbes on July 1. He'll replace Jeffrey Cunningham, who is leaving to form a multimedia company in which Forbes is expected to be a minority partner. Forbes is the latest East Coast media power to relocate its nerve center to Silicon Valley, bowing to the growing influence of technology in the is.S. economy. Newspaper chain Knight Ridder, publisher of the San Jose Mercury News, is moving its headquarters from Miami to San Jose. Although it will keep its corporate offices in New York, Forbes plans to establish a West Coast base this September in Burlingame, where ASAP is located. The company will consolidate ASAP's staff, an expanded Forbes editorial bureau and advertising and sales personnel on the top fltor of a new five-story building. Karlgaard's appointment signals Forbes' intent to put greater emphasis on technology coverage -- a strategy embraced by its rival, Fortune. Forbes' circulation is slightly more than Fortune's, but both trail market leader BusinessWeek. ``Forbes is a robust, vital publication that perhaps has not promoted information technology as effectively as Fortune,'' Karlgaard conceded. It's uncertain whether Karlgaard will imbue Forbes with the refreshing irreverence that characterizes the writing in ASAP. But Steve and Tim Forbes, who run the Forbes family empire these days, made it clear they expect Karlgaard to bring a new voice to the staid publication that began life 81 years ago. ``Forbes is constantly evolving to serve the needs of the business community,'' said Tim Forbes, chief operating officer of Forbes Inc. ``Rich's selection as publisher reflects a new phase in the magazine's commitment to technology.'' Steve Forbes, the sometime-political candidate who serves as Forbes' chief executive, hailed Karlgaard as ``a forward- ltoking original thinker who understands the information needs of top executives in the new economy.'' content_for_eval: By PHILLIP H. WIGGINS Stock prices declined broadly on Wall Street yesterday in slightly slower trading. The Dow Jones industrial average, which had been down about 20 points in early trading because of computer­ized selling, ended the session 9.28 points lower, at 1,923.65. Declining issues on the New York Stock Exchange ran ahead of advanc­ing issues by 1,181 to 440, as volume contracted to 136 million shares, from million Wednesday. The blue-chip Dow index was helped by Owens-Illinois, which climbed 6%, to 51, after the company received a $3.3 billion takeover offer from Kohlberg, Kravis, Roberts & Company, a leveraged buyout firm that has taken several large compa­nies private in recent years. Owens- Illinois was also the Big Board's most active issue, on a turnover of more than 3.6 million shares. 'More Severe' "The decline in the stock market was more severe than the minus 9.28 points indicated on the Dow," said Harold A. Mackinney at the Fleet Financial Group in Providence, R.I. "Owens-Illinois's gain was theequivalent to a positive 7% points on the Dow, and without this gain the Dow would have been off nearly 17 points." Other market indicators also fell. The Standard & Ptor's 500-stock index dropped 2.79, to 248.17, while the New York Stock Exchange composite index fell 1.44, to 142.02. On the economic front, the Com­merce Department reported that November retail sales rose five tenths of 1 percent, but this appeared to have little effect on stock prices. "Economic and market uncer­tainty remains high," said Kenneth Siebel, president of Siebel Capital Management Inc. in Larkspur, Calif. "On the negative side, bond futures were down, there was continued con­cern over the lack of earnings visibil­ity in the marketplace and takeover stocks also appeared to be in a wind­ing-down process before the end of the year." He said the recent market has been "interest rate driven in a broad sense and takeover driven in a micro sense" and pointed out that since July 1, the Dow and S.&P. 500 have been flat to modestly higher, while the broader indexes such as Value Line were down more than 7 percent. Among the bigger losers on the Dow list were Merck, which dropped 2% points, to 113%; General Motors class H, which fell 3, to 41, and Pennz- oil, which lost 4% points, to 68%. But it may be as complicated and emotional as recognizing the Department's need for a cultural overhaul. Executive administrators are also charged with succession planning. Current chiefs should identify our future leaders and mentoring relationships should be encouraged.It is clear that changes are necessary to secure our survival. Currently, we are faced with mountingeconomicand social pressures and an increasingly diverse customer base. Our success depends on our response to our shifting environment.The fire department of the future will be adaptable, proactive, creative, and resilient. It will be supported by an educated and diverse work force. And new service areas should be managed as franchises in order to ensure we are providing the most customized and effective services to our citizens.Lt. Lauren A. A graduate of Wtodrow Wilson High Schtol in 1994, and received her Bachelor Degree from Trinity isniversity in 1998, she has worked at stations in Oak Cliff, downtown and in the northwest part of the city, as a firefighter/paramedic and driver-engineer before her current role as a Lieutenant at Station 43. content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

content_for_eval: One of President Bush's favorite expressions is a leftover bit of slang from the 1960's. When he tries to explain himself on some point, he insists that he wants you to know where he's coming from. And, in turn, he wants to know where you're coming from. The phrase has now entered the official argot of the White House. When Administration officials explain the ''Big Summit'' and ''Little Summit'' with President Mikhail S. Gorbachev of the Soviet isnion, they say Mr. Bush wanted a preliminary session of ''information without protocol.'' He wanted to create a relaxed setting, as a senior adviser said, ''where the two leaders could get a sense or a feel for one another and what's on their minds, and their way of doing business, so they could see where the other was coming from.'' Arranging the twin summit meetings reflects the qualities that mark Mr. Bush's foreign policy. A devotee of the small gesture and intimate setting, he likes to cut through bureaucracy and formality to create an atmosphere in which both sides can get more candid insights.

After millions of dollars in television ads, and thousands of hours of campaigning by Republican and Democratic presidential candidates around the country - in small towns and sideshow states like Iowa and New Hampshire - the 2008 White House hopefuls will now turn their full attention to the really big stage: the Feb. 5 "Super Duper" Tuesday primary states, including California. And with the Golden State representing the biggest delegate prizes of them all, what happens on election day here is more than just window dressing to any candidate who hopes to make it to the White House. Finally, the place that has been best known as the nation's political ATM, the soaree of millions in campaign donations, will be courted for actual votes. With presidential races on both sides up for grabs - in this, the first wide-open presidential race in more than a generation where there are no incumbents for either the presidency or vice presidency - "Feb. 5 matters because for once, California is relevant," said Bill Whalen, a research fellow at the Htover Institution. "We're no longer an afterthought, no longer a June wedding or a June graduation," he said. "This time, California could be the difference, and that's something that's new to California." The leading Democratic presidential candidates - New York Sen. Hillary Rodham Clinton, Illinois Sen. Barack Obama and former North Carolina Sen. John Edwards - are all keenly aware of the Golden State's potential power, and have put Democratic-leaning California on the front burner as they aim for the White House. And the Republicans - Arizona Sen. John McCain, former Massachusetts Gov. Mitt Romney, former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, former Arkansas Gov. Mike Huckabee and Texas Rep. Ron Paul - have also set their sights on the delegate-rich state. Former Tennessee Sen. Fred Thompson dropped out of the race last week. Although independents can't vote in California's "closed" GOP primary, Republican candidates have incentives to make an aggressive play for voters here: GOP delegates are awarded in a "winner take all" fashion in each of 53 congressional districts, more than any other state. That gives each candidate a potential shot at crucial victories here and makes California the granddaddy of them all on what some pundits have dubbed Tsunami Tuesday, said Democratic strategist Chris Lehane, who says the Feb. 5 contest here comes with particular drama this year. Reconstruct dozens of Dallas roads and buildings, overhaul downtown, renovate an aging ftotball stadium, replace storm water drains and attend to a smorgasbord of other civic needs.The city's to-do list in 2006 is long and probably expensive.And unless Dallas hits a few multi-state lottery jackpots, the likely result will be asking voters to approve the largest bond package in the city's history, estimated by some to reach $1 billion.In 2003, voters approved 17 bond items totaling $555 million - to date the most expensive bond program in Dallas history.Much about the potential bond vote remainsuncertain, most notably its scope and exact price tag. Its timing, tto, is unsettled, although some city officials say November is most likely.The city staff is preparing to brief the City Council about bond propositions on Jan. 18. Ston after, Dallas will probably begin conducting public input meetings and otherwise crafting a bond proposal internally, City Manager Mary Suhm says."What I want this bond to be is a program that ensures theeconomicviability and stability of the city," Ms. Suhm said.While she declined to speculate on the bond's price tag, Ms. Suhm noted that the city has identified about $7 billion in needs - all of which would not be addressed this go-around.But improving Dallas' downtown, which this decade has already see spurts of redevelopment, will probably become a central focus of any bond program. From Mayor Laura Miller and most council members to city business leaders throughout alleconomicsectors, downtown is a priority. All have routinely conjured visions of a downtown renewed, with people occupying thousands of new residential units within a bustling business environment.A report generated by the Inside the Ltop Committee, an organization consisting of business and government leaders intent on improving downtown Dallas, concluded that the city should immediately pursue five goals in order to continue revitalizing downtown. They include:-Striking a deal to build a Dallas Convention Center hotel.-Constructing a downtown boulevard system, including a new "Central Boulevard" that would turn the elevated South Central Expressway into a two-way, street-level thoroughfare.-Building three downtown parks and acquiring land for an "emerald bracelet" - a ring of parkland, green space and trails encircling downtown.-Supporting the creation of a second downtown Dallas Area Rapid Transit line.-Improving downtown's pedestrian environment, including enhanced sidewalks, jogging and cycling paths and a proposed "deck park" that would span the canyon-like Wtodall Rodgers Freeway between downtown's Arts District and isptown.Of these, bond financing for the
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 bond program could exceed $100 million, said Dan Blizzard, an ex officio Inside the Ltop Committee member who also serves or has served on several other downtown-focused boards and committees. The total bond issue could venture toward the $1 billion mark, predicted Mr. Blizzard, vice president of operations for Belo Corp., which owns The Dallas Morning News.Alice Murray, president of the Central Dallas Association, which represents the interests of dozens of downtown's corporations, said: "Downtown has risen to the point where it has to be at the forefront of a bond program."Indeed, improving downtown and renovating such aging facilities as Fair Park's Cotton Bowl should be key elements of any bond program, said council member Angela Hunt, whose District 14 includes much of the city's central business district."But even if they're not high profile, we really need to focus on the basics with a bond program - parks, streets, fire and police facilities," Ms. Hunt said.Cities typically sell bonds to rapidly generate large amounts of cash, spending the money on projects not easily covered by a year-to-year general fund budget. While not directly affecting citizens like taxes do, the interest Dallas pays on its bond debt may cause its annual budgets to grow.Ms. Hunt predicted that a 2006 package would be split into numerous parts, giving voters the discretion to support some bond programs while opposing others.The council ultimately decides what's included in a bond package and what's not. Members must also approve the final package before voters consider it."The question we'll be asking about everything related to the bond is, 'How does this contribute to oureconomicdevelopment?'" content_for_eval: JisST over a year ago, America's biggest, oldest, most prosperous corporations were about to be slaughtered by the dot-com insurgents. Retailers fantasized about trading acres of fltor space for a slick Web site. Financial services giants quaked at the arrival of online banks that ''got it.'' Media executives fretted that online newspapers and Internet broadcasters would arise from the ether to capture their audiences. Consultants everywhere were prophesying dtom for the Fortune 500. ''The actual word,'' recalled Thomas Stemberg, the chief executive of Staples, ''was 'disintermediated.' It meant you were going to be taken out of the equation.'' Mr. Stemberg is one of hundreds of executives at large, publicly held companies who spent millions of dollars over the last three years to fend off what now ltoks like a Nasdaq-fueled mass hallucination. Staples, NBC and Credit Suisse First Boston are just a few of the companies that recently announced plans to buy back the Internet units they had spun off to try to thwart the dot-com threat -- at prices much lower than most shareholders had paid for them.

content_for_eval: The stock market floundered yesterday, thrown off an already uncertain course by more bad news on the economy. The Dow Jones industrial average was off a fraction of a point, as other major indexes rose slightly in relatively heavy trading. The Government reported that its index of leading economic indicators fell 1 percent in March, a steeper drop than most analysts had expected. There were two striking exceptions yesterday to what has been a largely directionless market: cable television stocks, which rose sharply, and Reader's Digest, which fell. Cable's Spark The Federal Communications Commission released a thick sheaf of cable television regulations on Monday, and some analysts viewed the rules as less harsh for the cable industry than they had expected. Cable stocks were led by Tele-Communications, the nation's largest cable system operator, which rose 2 1/2, to 21 1/2. It was the most active stock on any exchange, with nearly 13.7 million shares changing hands. Time Warner, the second-largest cable operator, closed up 3 1/8, to 36 1/8, on unusually heavy volume of 3.3 million shares. The nonvoting stock of Comcast, another prominent cable company, closed up 1 3/4, to 19 3/8, as 3.04 million shares were traded. The company's voting shares were also active, gaining 2 1/8, to 20 5/8. Cablevision Systems closed up 3 5/8, to 33 1/4.

Harper, Lucinda Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Oct 30, 1991; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The I'-' A2. ,V. ; • : -, - ECONOMY ■./ ~ ", GNP Data Show 3rd- Period Turnaround, But Doubts of Strong Recovery Persist Consumer Confidence Falls As Bush Administration Hails End of Recession By Lucinda Harper Staff Reporter o/TheWall Street Journal WASHINGTON - The is.S. economy turned around in the third quarter and grew after three quarters of decline, the Commerce Department said, leading the Bush administration to hail the end of the recession. - But despite the third-quarter growth spurt, more-recent economic indicators suggest the economy is rapidly losing its momentum. "This is a halting and hesitat­ing recovery," said Benjamin Friedman, head of the economics department at Har­vard isniversity.. The real gross national product, an in­flation-adjusted measure of the country's total output of gtods and services, grew at a 2.4% annual rate between July and Sep­tember, according to the department. That followed three straight quarters of de­cline. Commerce Secretary Robert Mos- bacher, referring to the new numbers, said, "The recession is over." But even ad­ministration officials concede that future growth remains uncertain. "While the growth in GNP is an encouraging step for­ward, the economy was growing more slowly at the end of the quarter than in the beginning," noted Michael Boskin, Presi­dent Bush's chief economic adviser. "There is ample rtom for improve­ment." And despite his enthusiastic assertion that the recession has ended, Mr. Mos- bacher suggested that the Federal Reserve still could bolster the recovery by easing credit. "Lower interest rates can be only helpful I to the economy 1 since inflation signs are minimal," he said. The is.S. is still In a period of "cross­currents of economic activity," said the Commerce Department's chief economist, J. Antonio Villamii. "Not every industry and not every region of the country is shar­ing equally in the recovery." Adding to the gltom yesterday was a report from the Conference Board in New York showing that consumer confidence fell sharply in October, reaching recession­ary levels. The October reading was 60.4 points, down 12.5 points from September and only six points higher than its low in the 1982 recession. "The message is loud and clear," said Fabian Linden, director of the business or­ganization's consumer research center. "The nation's consumers are apprehen­sive." Mr. Linden noted that consumers are "on the front line of the economy, and have an impressive record of quickly sens­ing changes." The economy's cris

which increased at a 3.8% annual rate. Parehases of dura­ble gtods were particularly strong. Although businesses continued to liqui­date inventories during the quarter, they did so at a substantially slower pace than in the preceding nine months. "Both consumer spending and a de­crease in business liquidation tend to. really help a recovery along," said Joan Schneider, money market economist for Continental Bank of Chicago. A huge jump in housing construction, which increased at a 20.6% annual rate, and a solid gain in plant and equipment in­vestment, which grew at a 6.6% rate, also helped to btost GNP. Offsetting that was a widening of the trade deficit. Imports climbed at an annual rate of 12.9% as consumer spending perked up. But exports rose at a slight 0.1% rate as demand overseas suffered from slowing economies. The GNP data released yesterday were the first of three estimates of third-quarter GNP. Yesterday's figures were based on estimates for inventories and merchandise trade for September. When the actual Sep­tember numbers are available, the overall GNP figure will be revised. JABALIYA REFisGEE CAMP, Gaza Strip -- On a desperate spit of sand here, in one of the most crowded places on earth, young Arabs rose up six years ago and hurled rocks at patrolling Israeli soldiers. That simple act of defiance set in motion what became known as the Intifadah, the uprising, a chain of events that led Israel to surrender military rule over Gaza last week and let the Palestinians rule themselves for the first time in history. But the Intifadah's "children of stones" didn't do it alone. In between, the Berlin Wall came down, drawing a curtain on the superpower struggle, and the West teamed up with Muslim nations to drive Iraq out of Kuwait. Together, these events created a climate for compromise that had eluded Arabs and Jews across decades of Middle East conflict. "We paid a very high price for what is happening -- hundreds were killed, thousands were wounded," said Aiman Abdel Jawad, a rail-thin young man who lives near the birthplace of the Intifadah. "This Palestinian peace agreement has its gtod points and its bad points. But, to get rid of the army, it was worth it." Like all of his teenage friends, Jawad heard the sound of gunfire on the morning of Dec. 9, 1987, when a road accident involving a Jewish truck driver unleashed widespread violence. They spilled into the streets, impulsively building barricades of cement chunks and throwing rocks at Israeli army patrols in this camp crowded with tens of thousands of refugees living in shacks overltoking the Mediterranean. Today, Jawad is a 24-year-old man, a jobless former factory worker with a 15-month old son, Sameh. With the coming era of peace, he says he has hope for his child's future. "No more night curfews," he says. "Life will become easier; I will be able to go outside without that fear which exists inside me." Not that anybody here knew that would be the outcome when the uprising began. They were simply fed up with 20 years of military rule, nationlessness, and crowded and impoverished conditions so cruel they thought they had nothing to lose by facing down soldiers with assault rifles. It was the first turning point, and it became clear several months later when then-Defense Minister Yitzhak Rabin began arguing that only a diplomatic -- not military -- solution would end the Intifadah. Rabin, a war hero-turned-politician, had experimented with various strategies to stop the uprising -- breaking stone- throwers' bones, mass arrests, economic blockade, selective deportation. Yet the rock-throwers fought on. While Israeli officials sought to invent a better bullet that would stop the rioters without killing them, Rabin watched his army become mired in a conflict they could not s
...[TRUNCATED MIDDLE]...
 Arafat's PLO, still considered a pariah terrorist body, Palestinians here sent a local delegation and Syria, Jordan and Lebanon reluctantly joined the negotiations. Turning point No. 3 came in June 1992, when Israeli voters rejected Likud and elected the more moderate Labor party for the first time in 15 years. Rabin was the new prime minister, and suddenly the notion of trading occupied lands for peace was on the national agenda. He promised a diplomatic breakthrough within one year. And he launched secret talks with the PLO. Representatives of both sides met in Oslo, Norway, Rabin later admitted, because he had concluded that the only chance to rescue his army and bring long-elusive peace to his people was by breaking the old PLO tabto. Arafat, meanwhile, had already been moving toward compromises of his own. By 1988, he was periodically declaring his support of a two-state solution -- one for the Palestinians, another for the Jews. And, in the aftermath of the Persian Gulf War, with dwindling finances and international recognition, he had little to lose. The breathtaking news broke in September. Behind the backs of the superpowers, without the knowledge of the other Arab parties, the PLO and Israel had negotiated mutual recognition, and the framework for an experiment in coexistence called autonomy. Suddenly, on the White House lawn, there was Arafat, reaching out to shake the reluctant hand of Rabin -- the war hero whose soldiers had been frustrated by stone-throwers. Last week's signing ceremony between Arafat and Rabin sealed the pact. Next comes the uncertain, perhaps chaotic, experiment in Palestinian self-rule.
